title: #eng-oncall: 1739563228
preview:
Rafael Mendes: on-call handoff for audit exporter incident from earlier (lag spike / downstream 429s). capturing what to watch + toggles. cc @Aisha Bello (taking primary)

Redwood Incident Bot: Incident INC-4471 status changed: MITIGATED → MONITORING

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- root cause: Splunk HEC rate-limiting (429) on 2 beta tenants → retries/backoff built up queue → export lag peaked ~38m
- mitigation: paused Splunk adapter for affected tenants + reduced batch size + verified no DLQ growth
- current: lag back < 5m; retry rate normalizing

Rafael Mendes: Dashboards to keep open tonight:
1) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Overview (lag + throughput)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-overview
   Panels to watch:
   - "p95 export lag (by destination)"
   - "events exported/sec (by tenant)"
   - "checkpoint age"
2) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Destinations
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-destinations
   Panels:
   - "adapter HTTP status codes" (look for sustained 429/403)
   - "retry attempts" + "backoff seconds"
3) Queue/Storage dashboard (exporter internal)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-queues
   Panels:
   - "pending batches" (should not trend up)
   - "DLQ depth" (should be ~0; anything non-zero means investigate)

Aisha Bello: got it. what are the paging thresholds we’re using right now?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE draft (we used these in the incident):
- page if p95 export lag > 15m for 10m (any destination) OR > 30m for 5m (critical)
- page if delivery success rate < 99.5% over 30m (per adapter)
- ticket-only if 429s elevated but lag < 10m
(Sean can confirm if we’re tweaking, but this is what alert rules are set to as of today)

Sean Gallagher: confirming. alert rules match. note: we loosened 429 warning to ticket-only after the noise last week.

Rafael Mendes: Toggles / controls (use sparingly; prefer tenant-scoped first):
A) Pause exports per destination type (safe):
- control-plane admin: `POST /internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/pause` with body `{"destination":"splunk"}`
- UI (Console → Audit Exports → Actions → Pause) works if you have the SRE role
This stops new sends but keeps checkpoints; lag metric will stop increasing but backlog remains.

B) Global kill switch (last resort):
- feature flag: `siem_audit_export_beta_enabled` (tenant allowlist). DO NOT disable globally unless we’re melting down.
- if you must, flip via internal admin tool: https://admin.redwood.internal/flags/siem_audit_export_beta_enabled

C) Backpressure safety / auto-pause:
- exporter has `maxLagAutoPauseMinutes=60` (from PR-1119). If lag hits this, it auto-pauses the destination and emits an error.
- when it triggers, you’ll see alert "destination auto-paused" + log line `AUTO_PAUSE_MAX_LAG`.

Aisha Bello: if we pause, what’s the expected customer impact message?

Rafael Mendes: "exports temporarily paused due to downstream rate limiting; no audit events deleted; once resumed exporter will backfill from last checkpoint; duplicates possible (at-least-once)". don’t promise exact timestamps.

Logan Wright: adding: if customer asks "missing events", point them to dedup via `event_id` on their side. at-least-once semantics.

Rafael Mendes: Quick triage checklist if lag starts climbing again:
1) Identify adapter causing it:
   - check "p95 export lag by destination" + "HTTP status codes"
2) If 429s from Splunk:
   - confirm which tenant(s): panel "429s by tenant"
   - reduce pressure first (tenant pause Splunk) rather than global
3) If 403s from Datadog:
   - usually API key scope / wrong site (datadoghq.com vs datadoghq.eu). see SUP-18497 notes.
4) If S3 errors:
   - look for `AccessDenied` or KMS issues; often rotated role trust or KMS key policy.

Rafael Mendes: Commands / where to look:
- exporter logs (prod):
  `kubectl -n audit-exporter logs deploy/audit-exporter -c exporter --since=30m | rg "ERROR|429|AUTO_PAUSE|DLQ"`
- check tenant config state:
  `curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://control-plane.redwood.internal/internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/status`
  (status shows last_success_ts, last_error, paused=true/false, checkpoint_ts)

Aisha Bello: what about DLQ handling? any gotchas?

Rafael Mendes: DLQ gotchas:
- DLQ depth should be ~0. if it starts growing, do NOT spam replay.
- replay tool (guardrailed) lives in exporter image:
  `audit-exporter replay --tenant <id> --destination splunk --from-dlq --max-batches 50`
- always check downstream health first; replay while Splunk still 429’ing just makes things worse.
- replay will create duplicates; tell customer to dedup by `event_id`.

Sean Gallagher: +1. if DLQ > 1000 batches or growing steadily, page secondary and consider pausing exports to prevent runaway.

Rafael Mendes: When to involve Security (Marcus/Kevin):
- any sign of export sending to an unrecognized host / destination tampering (possible exfil)
- if logs contain unexpected sensitive fields (PII) or raw request payloads (should be redacted)
- if we suspect credentials exposure (Splunk HEC token / Datadog key showing up in logs, even partially)
- if customer asks for compliance language re: retention vs export responsibilities beyond the FAQ

Marcus Lin: +1. also loop us in if you’re asked to widen egress allowlist for a non-standard SIEM endpoint. we need threat-model review.

Rafael Mendes: Notes from today’s incident specifics:
- affected tenants: 2 (beta) using Splunk HEC; both had customer-side limit set lower than expected
- we paused Splunk for tenant A for ~22m, then resumed after customer confirmed HEC throttle settings updated
- no evidence of dropped events in exporter; checkpoints monotonic; DLQ remained at 0
- watch for recurrence around top of hour (their SIEM pipelines seemed to burst)

Aisha Bello: thanks. who is the POC if I need customer comms?

Rafael Mendes: customer comms: route through TAM/CSM; for the two tenants today it was Liam O'Rourke + Miguel Santos. if urgent/outage, use #customer-success + incident channel.

Logan Wright: also FYI we deployed a small config change earlier: reduced default Splunk batch payload size (helps avoid 413/429 cascades). no rollback expected.

Sean Gallagher: alright. Aisha, page me if lag breaches 30m or if auto-pause triggers on more than 3 tenants.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’m on it. :saluting_face:

Rafael Mendes: handing off. I’ll stay available for ~30m if anything flares.

title: #eng-platform: 1739549012
preview:
Ethan Park: quick q before we lock the readiness APIs — who should be allowed to mutate node readiness state vs read-only? also need a plan for audit events when image channel/version changes for a pool.

Benji Okafor: +1. current proto has:
- `GET /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/readiness`
- `POST /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/events` (validation results)
- `PATCH /v1/fleet/nodes/{node_id}/readiness` (state transition)
Right now auth is just “internal service token”. needs tightening.

Jordan Lee: for state machine integrity: only ONE writer should be authoritative for `PATCH readiness`.
If multiple actors can set `Ready/Quarantined` directly, we’ll end up in weird transitions.

Ethan Park: agree. thinking:
- provisioning orchestrator is the only caller that can advance Allocated->Provisioning->Validating->Ready (and quarantine on policy)
- validation agent can only submit validation artifacts/results (not set state)
- internal UI + SRE tooling should be read-only

Fiona McCarthy: security POV: least privilege + auditable identity.
“internal token” without scopes isn’t going to fly once Private customers ask about change control.
We need explicit scopes/roles for read vs write.

Marcus Lin: +1. also make sure any write endpoint logs the actor identity + request id. we’ve had incidents where service A impersonated service B via shared token.

Hanae Suzuki: are we using the standard service-to-service authz middleware (OIDC svc tokens w/ claims) or rolling our own here?

Benji Okafor: control-plane uses the OIDC svc tokens already. we can enforce:
- `aud=redwood-control-plane`
- `svc=provisioning-orchestrator`
- `scopes=[fleet.readiness.write]`

Ethan Park: yeah, should reuse the same middleware. question: do we also allow Platform ops (humans) to mutate state for break-glass?

Harish Reddy: pls yes but behind break-glass. sometimes we need to move a node to Quarantined when automation is stuck and capacity ops is on fire.

Fiona McCarthy: break-glass is fine if:
1) it’s a separate role (no default)
2) MFA / elevated session
3) every action emits an audit event w/ reason

Jordan Lee: +1. but don’t allow “set arbitrary state”. allow a constrained operator action like `POST /v1/fleet/nodes/{id}:quarantine` and `POST ...:retryValidation`.
Avoid `PATCH state=Ready` from humans.

Benji Okafor: that’s cleaner. we can keep `PATCH readiness` private to orchestrator only.
Then add operator endpoints w/ explicit verbs.

Priya Natarajan: +1. keep state machine consistent. let humans trigger actions, not set final state.
Also we should make the API contract explicit for who can do what; otherwise internal console will get “temporary” write perms and never lose them.

Connor O'Brien: +1 from releases: image channel changes are basically releases. needs audit trail + we need to know who flipped candidate->stable and why.

Ethan Park: ok let’s talk audit events for image channel changes. where should this live?
We have a pool-level config: `image_channel` (candidate/stable) and `image_version`.

Benji Okafor: we can emit audit events on `PATCH /v1/fleet/pools/{pool_id}` when image fields change.
Event type: `fleet.pool.image_channel_changed` and `fleet.pool.image_version_changed`.

Marcus Lin: please include both old + new values in the audit event. and include correlation ids:
- `actor_type` (service|human)
- `actor_id` (svc name or user)
- `request_id`
- `change_ticket` (Linear/Jira)
- `commit_sha` if change originated from Terraform apply

Hanae Suzuki: also retention. audit logs need to go to the central audit sink (not just app logs). do we have an existing “audit-events” topic?

Benji Okafor: yep, `audit-events-prod` Kafka topic (same as KMS changes). we can emit a structured JSON event, and keep a copy pointer in DB for quick UI display.

Fiona McCarthy: careful on “copy in DB”. DB copy is fine but audit sink is source of truth. also make sure tamper-resistance: append-only.

Jordan Lee: question: if Terraform sets `image_channel=stable` but value is already stable, do we emit an event?

Connor O'Brien: for releases we usually *don’t* want no-op spam, but we *do* want an event when someone *attempts* a flip and it’s rejected.

Ethan Park: ok:
- emit audit event on successful change (old!=new)
- emit audit event on rejected change (403/validation fail) as `fleet.pool.image_change_rejected` w/ reason

Harish Reddy: +1. rejected reasons are super helpful for ops (“why didn’t it switch?”).

Benji Okafor: authz proposal:
READ:
- `fleet.readiness.read` for internal UI, SRE, support tooling
WRITE:
- `fleet.readiness.write` ONLY for `provisioning-orchestrator`
- `fleet.readiness.artifacts.write` for `node-validation-agent` to post validation results
OPS ACTIONS:
- `fleet.readiness.ops` for `:quarantine` / `:retryValidation`

Fiona McCarthy: looks good. add a guardrail that `node-validation-agent` can only write artifacts for its own node identity (if we have node identity) OR force it to go through orchestrator.

Benji Okafor: we don’t have strong node identity yet (no SPIFFE on workers today). safer: agent posts to orchestrator (in-cluster), orchestrator forwards to readiness service with its token.

Ethan Park: +1. keep readiness service perimeter small. agent -> orchestrator only.

Priya Natarajan: can we document this in the service doc + ADR? I don’t want to rediscover this when we onboard the next region.

Benji Okafor: yep. I’ll update service doc + add an “AuthZ” section w/ endpoint matrix.
Also will add audit events on pool patch (image fields) + include actor claims.

Redwood Deploy Bot: PR opened: redwood/pr-18503 "add audit events for image channel changes" (WIP)

Marcus Lin: please tag sec-eng for review on PR. also double-check we’re not logging secrets in audit event payload.

Hanae Suzuki: +1 and include the minimal set in payload. no full terraform plan dumps pls.

Ethan Park: action items:
- Benji: implement scoped authz + operator verb endpoints + audit event emission
- Fiona/Marcus: review event schema + retention requirements
- Connor: define required fields for change mgmt (ticket id, release train) so it shows up in rollout checklist

Benji Okafor: ack. will post the endpoint matrix here once I’ve got it in docs.

Harish Reddy: thanks. ops will be happy if we can quarantine without asking someone to temporarily grant admin on the readiness service :)

Priya Natarajan: ship it, but keep the blast radius tight. no broad write scopes for console.

title: #architecture: 1739520203
preview:
Ethan Park: need final eyes on ADR before we mark “ready for approval” today. main open item: make the decision points super explicit + document rejected alternatives (blue/green etc). anyone have last-minute concerns?

Talia Benson: +1 please. we keep getting the same questions in review: “why not blue/green” and “what exactly is rollback guaranteed to do.”

Connor O'Brien: read through the draft this morning. i think we need a crisp “Decision” section w/ bullets:
- in-place upgrade driven by generated upgrade plan artifact
- N-1 -> N only initially
- rollback is best-effort but deterministic within contract (and what’s out of scope)

Sean Gallagher: biggest thing for me is we don’t oversell rollback. should say: rollback is guaranteed to restore control-plane + config state to prior version *if* prechecks pass + backup completed + we haven’t crossed non-reversible migration boundary.

Michael Nguyen: on “non-reversible”: we should be very direct about schema migrations.
- default is backward-compatible migrations (additive) for N-1 -> N
- if we ever need a breaking migration, rollback becomes “restore-from-backup required” and we treat as manual / escalated path

Benji Okafor: +1. also include that we’re not relying on `helm rollback` as the mechanism. helm rollback alone doesn’t revert CRD changes / externalized state.

Rafael Mendes: yep. oncall perspective: we need a simple “rollback entry points” paragraph:
- rollback before CP cutover (safe)
- rollback after CP upgraded but before DP rollout (should be safe)
- rollback after DP changes (depends on migrations + workload)

Ethan Park: makes sense. ADR currently has “phases” but not the explicit contract language.

Quentin Harris: can we add the compatibility policy tie-in? i.e. upgrade plan generation is gated by policy evaluation, blocks unsupported jumps.

Connor O'Brien: yes please. reviewers keep asking “what stops someone from jumping 2 major versions.”

Naomi Feldman: from security side: rejected alternatives should mention why blue/green isn’t automatically “safer” for regulated on-prem. data duplication + two live control planes + key material handling. also cost.

Marcus Lin: also network: blue/green implies DNS / LB cutovers. in private deployments we can’t assume customer has automated traffic shifting, esp on-prem with static VIPs.

Allison Grant: observability note: in-place with plan gives us an auditable step log and we can emit structured events per step. blue/green cutover tends to look like a single flip unless we build extra instrumentation.

Sean Gallagher: also operationally, blue/green doubles failure modes (two clusters to keep healthy, drift, split-brain if control plane talks to wrong dp). i’d love that called out explicitly.

Talia Benson: ok but we should be fair: blue/green *does* reduce blast radius sometimes. so ADR should say “considered, rejected for v1 due to X” not “never.”

Ethan Park: agreed. section: “Rejected alternatives (for v1)” and include a “revisit when we have…” list.

Michael Nguyen: wording suggestion for migrations:
- “We optimize for forward-only additive migrations to preserve rollbackability via versioned deploy rollback + config restore.”
- “When not possible, rollback requires restore-from-backup; installer will surface ‘manual intervention required’ and stop.”

Rafael Mendes: +1 add explicit “stop points.” oncall needs clear criteria: when rollback is recommended vs when to escalate.

Connor O'Brien: also call out why “always rebuild cluster” isn’t viable: on-prem hardware procurement / scheduling + long drain times. plus customers don’t want new cluster IDs/certs every upgrade.

Benji Okafor: don’t forget “blue/green inside same cluster via namespaces” option. we looked at it: still needs CRD versioning, shared DB, shared LB => not actually isolated.

Ethan Park: yep. list of alternatives to capture:
1) full blue/green cluster swap
2) namespace-based blue/green
3) helm-only rollback
4) immutable “recreate everything” (tear down + reinstall)

Sean Gallagher: and for each, one paragraph on why it’s rejected. keep it short.

Quentin Harris: for #1, add: requires duplicate capacity (GPUs esp) + artifact mirrors for both stacks. for VPC customers maybe ok, for on-prem often impossible.

Naomi Feldman: for #3 helm-only rollback: add security angle: doesn’t roll back secrets refs / external KMS config changes, so could leave cluster in inconsistent auth state.

Marcus Lin: for blue/green: mention customer networking constraints: changing endpoint IPs / internal DNS propagation can take minutes-hours. can’t do safe cutover inside the upgrade SLO.

Allison Grant: “decision point” i want in ADR: we store upgrade plan + execution state as an auditable artifact (support bundle + audit log events). that’s a key differentiator vs ad-hoc scripts.

Connor O'Brien: +1 and tie to change-management gating: prechecks must pass, backups must succeed before any irreversible step.

Ethan Park: ok action items i’ll do now:
- add explicit “Decision” bullets
- add “Rollback contract” subsection (guarantees + stop points)
- add “Rejected alternatives (v1)” section w/ blue/green + others
- add “Revisit” criteria (ex: when we have traffic shifting primitives + dual-write migrations)

Sean Gallagher: can you also add a sentence: “rollback is a first-class command that consumes stored state; it is idempotent” ? reviewers keep asking.

Ethan Park: yes.

Talia Benson: timeline-wise, can we get the updated doc in ~1 hr so i can attach to the approval request?

Ethan Park: yep. will post link when updated.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: also please add a note that N-1 -> N is enforced by policy in the installer, not just “recommended.”

Quentin Harris: :thumbsup: i can review the policy wording once you paste.

Naomi Feldman: :thumbsup: ping me on the security bits.

Ethan Park: updated ADR draft is up w/ new sections. please review: rejected alternatives now includes blue/green + namespace-based + helm-only + recreate. link in drive: https://confluence.redwoodinference.local/x/ADR-Private-Upgrade-Plan-Rollback (same page)

Sean Gallagher: reading now. first pass looks good. “stop points” paragraph is what we needed.

Connor O'Brien: skimmed. +1. small nit: in “Revisit blue/green” add “when we can guarantee deterministic CP<->DP pairing during cutover” (avoid split brain).

Ethan Park: added.

Allison Grant: love the auditability callout. also added note re: emitting audit events per phase.

Talia Benson: looks good to me. moving to approval request w/ SRE + Release Eng.

Ethan Park: thanks all. marking ADR “final review complete” unless more comments come in.

title: #eng-platform: 1739473300
preview:
Allison Grant: heads up: for Private upgrade + rollback work, we need to finalize audit log events for the upgrade lifecycle. Compliance customers (esp. on-prem) are asking what we emit + required fields. Want to lock schema this week so installer + CP can align.

Kevin Osei: +1. For SIEM integrations, they keep asking for “who/what/when/where + outcome” + correlation ids. Also need a stable event name/version so parsers don’t break.

Irene Choi: question: are we emitting from the control plane only, or also directly from the installer CLI (since on-prem can be disconnected)?

Ethan Park: both, unfortunately. Control plane can emit canonical events for VPC deployments. For air-gapped/on-prem, installer must write locally + optionally forward to customer’s syslog/SIEM endpoint. We should keep payloads identical.

Naomi Feldman: +1 to identical payloads. Also: explicit guidance on redaction. We cannot leak secrets, tokens, KMS key material, or full config dumps in audit logs.

Allison Grant: agreed. Proposal: canonical set of lifecycle events:
- `private.upgrade.started`
- `private.upgrade.prechecks.completed`
- `private.upgrade.backup.completed`
- `private.upgrade.plan.applied` (or per-stage?)
- `private.upgrade.completed`
- `private.rollback.invoked`
- `private.rollback.completed`
- `private.upgrade.failed` / `private.rollback.failed`

Benji Okafor: do we need per-step events? could get noisy. But SRE might want to know “migration started/finished” explicitly.

Rafael Mendes: from oncall side, per-stage is enough if stage names are stable (control-plane, migrations, data-plane). But we do need migration duration + lock timeout incidents visible.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng POV: I’d like at least one event that includes the artifact digests (chart + images) so we can trace “what exactly ran” during an upgrade window.

Kevin Osei: required fields (minimum) for compliance packs I’ve seen:
- `event_name` (string)
- `event_version` (int)
- `occurred_at` (rfc3339)
- `actor.type` (user|service|system)
- `actor.id` (user id / service account)
- `actor.display_name` (if user)
- `actor.auth_type` (sso|api_key|kube_sa|local)
- `customer_org_id` / `tenant_id`
- `environment_id` (private env)
- `deployment_mode` (vpc|on_prem)
- `upgrade_id` (uuid)
- `from_version` / `to_version`
- `result` (success|failure|partial)
- `reason` / `error_code` (stable enum) + `error_summary`
- `correlation_id` (ties installer + CP)

Marcus Lin: add `source` fields so we can tell where it originated:
- `emitter` (control_plane|installer)
- `emitter_version` (installer version)
- `host_id` or `cluster_id` (hashed ok)
Also consider `remote_ip`/`user_agent` for control plane actions. For installer, maybe `workstation_id` optional.

Naomi Feldman: careful: IP + user agent can be considered personal data in some jurisdictions. For Private customers it’s usually okay but we should make it “best effort” and document it. Also: no raw kubeconfig paths, no command-line args echoed with secrets.

Allison Grant: makes sense. I’ll mark `remote_ip`/`user_agent` as optional and only for CP-emitted events. For installer events, we’ll include `operator_session_id` + `kube_context_name`? (maybe too revealing)

Irene Choi: I’d avoid kube context name in audit log. That ends up containing internal naming conventions. `cluster_uid` (k8s UID) hashed would be safer.

Ethan Park: +1. Also we need to represent prechecks + backups without dumping everything.
Maybe:
- `prechecks.summary`: { blocking_failed: N, warning_failed: N } (numbers)
- `prechecks.failed_checks`: list of check ids (e.g., `k8s.nodes_ready`, `storage.headroom`, `oidc.reachable`)
- `backup.type`: (csi_snapshot|ebs_snapshot|logical)
- `backup.snapshot_ids`: list of ids (or hashed?)

Kevin Osei: snapshot ids are usually fine, but not if they contain account ids. If AWS snapshot ID is just `snap-...`, ok.

Connor O'Brien: also need `artifacts`:
- `chart_version`
- `chart_digest` (or tarball sha256)
- `image_digests` (maybe top-level list)
- `bundle_digest` for air-gapped bundle
That’s basically what customers ask for during audits.

Benji Okafor: on the step granularity: if we add one for migrations:
- `private.upgrade.migrations.started`
- `private.upgrade.migrations.completed` with `duration_ms` + `lock_timeout_ms` + `retries`
That seems helpful + not too spammy.

Rafael Mendes: yes please. Also would like a field to capture “rollback recommended” vs “manual intervention required”. Support keeps asking for deterministic guidance.

Allison Grant: we can encode in failure event:
- `recommended_action` (rollback|retry|manual)
- `runbook_url` (static link)

Naomi Feldman: runbook_url is fine. Make sure it’s not a private Slack link.

Ethan Park: where does `upgrade_id` come from? Installer generates and passes to CP, or CP generates?

Irene Choi: for on-prem, installer must generate. For VPC, CP could generate but then installer needs it immediately for local log lines.

Allison Grant: propose installer generates `upgrade_id` always, includes it in API calls to CP. CP uses it as primary key for audit events + upgrade state. That keeps correlation simple.

Connor O'Brien: +1.

Kevin Osei: versioning: please don’t “tweak fields” without bumping `event_version`. SIEM parsers depend on this.

Marcus Lin: also we should sign or at least integrity-protect the local audit log? On-prem customers asked about tamper evidence.

Naomi Feldman: signing is probably Phase 2. For now: write append-only JSONL with rotation + recommend shipping to customer’s SIEM. Make sure we capture enough to prove “an upgrade happened” even if the upgrade fails midway.

Allison Grant: ack. For this milestone, we’ll do:
- JSONL file on installer host (path configurable)
- include `sequence_num` per upgrade_id so gaps are detectable
- include `previous_event_hash`? (might be too much right now)

Ethan Park: we already have PR draft for `upgrade start/stop` events on CP side (redwood/pr-29187). We can extend to include the fields above. I’ll sync with Michael on DB schema if needed.

Rafael Mendes: can we ensure failure events always include the last successful stage? That’s what we need during incident response.

Allison Grant: good call. Add:
- `stage` (string)
- `last_completed_stage` (string)

Irene Choi: also: include `maintenance_window_id` / `change_request_id` if provided (enterprises want linkage to approvals).

Connor O'Brien: yep. We can plumb from Terraform var / installer flag.

Kevin Osei: summarizing required fields (v1):
- core: event_name, event_version, occurred_at
- ids: customer_org_id, environment_id, upgrade_id, correlation_id
- versions: from_version, to_version
- origin: emitter, emitter_version
- actor: actor.type, actor.id, actor.display_name (optional), actor.auth_type
- outcome: result, error_code (optional), error_summary (optional), recommended_action (optional)
- context: deployment_mode, stage, last_completed_stage
- artifacts: bundle_digest (optional), chart_version, chart_digest, image_digests (optional list)
- checks/backups: prechecks.summary, prechecks.failed_checks (optional list), backup.type (optional), backup.snapshot_ids (optional list)
- approvals: change_request_id (optional)

Naomi Feldman: +1. Also include explicit note in docs: “audit logs are not application logs; no request payloads; no prompt/model inputs.”

Allison Grant: action items:
- Kevin: write a short schema doc + stable `error_code` enum proposal by Thu
- Ethan: update CP audit emitter (PR-29187 follow-up) to include `upgrade_id`, version fields, artifacts digests
- Irene: confirm installer local JSONL location + rotation defaults for on-prem
- Naomi/Marcus: quick privacy/security review of optional IP/UA + snapshot ids
I’ll start a lightweight spec in confluence and link here.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: also looping in Release Eng once schema is set; we’ll add to release gating checklist (must see start+complete events in staging).

Rafael Mendes: :+1: from SRE. Once we have events, we can build the “upgrade window” dashboard annotations off them.

Allison Grant: cool. Will post the spec link later today.

title: #architecture: 1739450020
preview:
Talia Benson: Kicking off a thread to coordinate the architecture review for route-level quantization enablement + fallback behavior. Need sign-off this week to keep the schema/API/routing work unblocked.

Talia Benson: ADR draft is here (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0137-Route-level-quantization-profile-enablement

Talia Benson: Proposed review slot: Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. Attendees: Platform (Priya/Ethan/Mina/Ravi/Jordan), Runtime (Mateo/Noah), Console (Dylan/Chloe), Security/RBAC (Naomi/Marcus), PM (Marissa). If you can’t make it, pls comment on the ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. Main thing from Product side: must be explicitly opt-in per route for v1, no global “tenant default enables everything.” Also needs “easy rollback” story that Support can execute.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks Talia. Read through ADR quickly. Overall direction seems right. I want to make sure we’re crisp on:
- what exactly is stored in route config (profile_id vs profile_ref vs alias)
- where compatibility is enforced (policy engine vs runtime)
- deterministic resolution (same request config => same profile)

Ethan Park: I can make Thu.
Question: ADR says “route references a profile ref string” and examples show both `recommended` and a versioned id. We should avoid anything that can change under customer feet.

Mina Haddad: +1. If we allow `recommended` alias, then “recommended” moving to a new version changes behavior without an explicit config write. That’s basically hidden rollout.

Ravi Menon: Agree. My suggestion: v1 should only allow explicit versioned `quant_profile_id` (immutable). If we need an alias later, treat it as a separate field with “pinning” semantics.

Jordan Lee: Determinism hat on: any alias that’s resolved at request time based on “latest” breaks determinism unless we snapshot it into resolved policy at write time.

Noah Patel: Runtime side: we can handle either, but if policy engine resolves to an explicit immutable id before the request hits the runtime, it’s cleaner. Runtime should never call “what’s the newest profile” mid-request.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Runtime should consume resolved result: {enabled, profile_id, reason_if_fallback}. Everything else belongs in control plane.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX question: customers will ask “just use recommended.” But we can still show “recommended” in UI and when they click enable, we write the pinned versioned ID. That gives UX simplicity without the hidden change risk.

Chloe Martin: That’s workable for backend too. Console can call a “preview” endpoint that returns which profile id would be pinned + compatibility warnings.

Naomi Feldman: For audit/compliance: we need the audit event to include the exact pinned profile id and diff. “Enabled recommended” is not sufficient for change control.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clarity on who can enable/disable this. Ideally a separate permission (route config write but with elevated capability). At minimum, record actor + route + old/new profile.

Talia Benson: Sounds like we’re converging on: UI may say “recommended” but route config stores immutable `quant_profile_id` (or `default`). I’ll add that as a decision point in ADR.

Priya Natarajan: On compatibility enforcement: ADR currently says policy engine checks model/hardware/seq length and falls back to baseline. Runtime also has guardrails.
We should decide: which layer is authoritative? (I’d like policy engine to be authoritative; runtime should still validate to protect itself.)

Jordan Lee: Agree. Policy eval should produce a single resolved decision, including fallback, to avoid oscillation.
Also: if runtime independently decides to fallback, we must avoid a feedback loop where policy keeps trying and runtime keeps rejecting.

Ravi Menon: +1 on “no oscillation.” I propose we include a `resolution_outcome` in the resolved policy: `APPLIED | FALLBACK_INCOMPATIBLE | DISABLED | KILL_SWITCH`.
Runtime logs it but shouldn’t re-resolve.

Noah Patel: Runtime still has to protect against mismatched artifacts / corrupted profile / unsupported kernel path. In those cases we should fail closed to baseline and emit metrics.
But we should NOT attempt to re-query policy. Just do local fallback.

Mateo Alvarez: Exactly. Runtime local fallback is last resort; also needs a counter so SRE can alert when it happens.

Connor O'Brien: From release/oncall POV: we need a clear kill switch behavior in the ADR:
- global kill switch disables application everywhere (even if route is enabled)
- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

(DeployBot): Reminder: Architecture Review "Quantization Profile Enablement + Fallbacks" scheduled Thu 11:00 PT (Zoom link in calendar invite).

title: #eng-platform: 1739408123
preview:
Amara Diallo: quick q for RBAC v2: where do we want to enforce authz checks long-term — gateway vs service? I’m leaning gateway because consistency + perf, but worried about bypass/internal calls. Looking for a decision we can turn into an ADR.

Jordan Lee: tl;dr IMO service must be source of truth. Gateway-only is too easy to bypass (internal service-to-service, console talking directly in some flows, private installer hitting CP). Gateway can be defense-in-depth / early reject.

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane endpoints need checks in-process. We already have internal jobs calling the same handlers w/o going thru gateway.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also authn context differs. Gateway sees external identity, but service has to validate org/project/env scope anyway. If we push checks to gateway only, services will start trusting headers and that’s… spooky.

Amara Diallo: yup, the “trust an X-Authz-Result header” pattern makes me nervous. But I still want the gateway to block obvious stuff to keep load down + standardize error payloads.

Priya Natarajan: think of it as layers:
- gateway: coarse, fast, deny-by-default for annotated routes; consistent 403 semantics; metrics
- service: authoritative check w/ same shared library; required for any state-changing + sensitive reads
If there’s ever divergence, service wins.

Marcus Lin: security POV: checks MUST exist at the data boundary (service) or it’s not defensible in SOC2 / enterprise review. Gateway checks are great but not sufficient evidence.

Allison Grant: from observability side, having checks in service makes audit logging + “who/what/why denied” easier to capture with the action + resolved scope. Gateway doesn’t always know final resource.

Amara Diallo: ok. then performance question: do we expect every service to call a centralized authz service? or embed policy eval lib?

Jordan Lee: this is basically ADR-015. I’m in “embedded evaluator + shared policy bundle + safe caching” camp.
Central service = single point of failure + latency tax + hard in Private (customers don’t want a bunch of moving pieces).

Ethan Park: also network hops in private/onprem are unreliable. A central authz svc outage would brick the control plane.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: but embedded evaluator needs up-to-date policy + bindings. what’s the caching / invalidation plan? that’s where we keep getting stuck.

Logan Wright: if we do embedded, we need:
1) policy bundle versioning (immutable) + signed artifact
2) bindings stored in CP DB (or cache) w/ change notification
3) evaluator client cache keyed by (subject, action, scope, policy_version, bindings_version)
Then you can do bounded TTL + fast invalidation on role changes.

Jordan Lee: exactly. I don’t want “TTL only” because role removals should take effect quickly. Suggest:
- short TTL (e.g., 30s) for allow decisions
- shorter TTL (e.g., 5-10s) for deny decisions (to avoid annoying “still denied” after grant)
- hard invalidation when we see bindings_version bump for that org/project

Ines Carvalho: careful: scope is not always just org. For v2 we have org/project/env(/cluster for private). So invalidation needs to be scoped, otherwise one role edit flushes everything for an org and kills cache hit rate.

Amara Diallo: gateway cache: are we comfortable caching at gateway too? (it will call evaluator like everyone else)

Jordan Lee: yes, but only via the same evaluator client library. No bespoke gateway-only cache.

Marcus Lin: also important: cache must be scoped to tenant. Obvious, but we’ve been bitten before with global caches. Include org_id in the cache key always. :grimacing:

Allison Grant: + include region/cluster in key for Dedicated/Private so you don’t cross-contaminate.

Ethan Park: failure modes:
- if policy bundle fetch fails -> keep last-known-good (LKG) for N minutes, alert
- if bindings store read fails -> should we fail closed? at least for writes

Kaitlyn Nguyen: we need to be explicit about fail-open vs fail-closed. My proposal:
- write operations: fail closed (503 if evaluator unavailable vs 403)
- read operations: default fail closed too, but we can consider some non-sensitive reads fail-open only if explicitly annotated (probably not for v2)

Marcus Lin: agree, fail-open should be rare and very deliberate. If anything, we prefer 503 “authz unavailable” over incorrectly allowing.

Amara Diallo: gateway behavior if evaluator errors: return 503? (not 403). For consistency we can do:
- 403 = explicit deny
- 503 = authz system unavailable

Allison Grant: yes, and we should emit distinct metrics. We’ll need dashboards:
- authz_eval_latency_ms p50/p95/p99
- authz_decision_total{allow|deny|error}
- deny reason codes counts
- cache hit rate

Logan Wright: + add trace annotation so we can see where the latency is (gateway vs service). Otherwise we’ll argue about it forever.

Benji Okafor: another angle: even if gateway blocks, service still needs to protect itself against direct access (internal mesh, cronjobs, etc). So we should treat gateway check as optional optimization.

Amara Diallo: what about internal service-to-service calls where user context is propagated? ex: console -> API -> CP -> subservice.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: we should propagate a “subject” in a signed internal token (our existing service auth token) and do authz at the leaf service using that subject + action + scope. No “I already checked at gateway” short-circuiting.

Jordan Lee: +1. If we add an optimization later, it should be something like a short-lived signed authz assertion with policy_version embedded. But that’s v3 territory and easy to get wrong.

Priya Natarajan: keep it simple for v2:
- enforce in service
- optionally enforce in gateway too
- shared library + evaluator client cache
- clear semantics for errors
Let’s document and move.

rbac-bot: Reminder: ADR-015 draft exists in Confluence but not finalized. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-015+Centralized+policy+evaluation+vs+embedded (access may be restricted)

Connor O'Brien: release angle: if we rely on a central authz svc, rollouts become scary (can’t stage per-service). Embedded w/ policy bundle versioning is much easier to roll out behind flags.

Amara Diallo: makes sense. One more: streaming endpoints. gateway sees the upgrade, service streams tokens. where do we check?

Ethan Park: check at stream start in the service and bind the stream to the validated subject+scope. Don’t allow mid-stream “permission changed” to cut it off for v2 (too complex). But if the initial check is missing, that’s exactly how we get bypasses.

Marcus Lin: + add regression tests specifically for streaming routes. We already saw how easy it is to forget the check when it’s a different handler path.

Amara Diallo: ok action items:
- Jordan/Priya: can you update ADR-015 w/ this framing (service is source of truth; gateway optional defense-in-depth; embedded evaluator + safe caching; 403 vs 503 semantics)?
- I’ll align gateway middleware to call the shared evaluator client and standardize error responses.

Jordan Lee: yep. I’ll add a section on caching + invalidation triggers and explicitly call out “no trusting forwarded authz headers”.

Priya Natarajan: I’ll review + make sure we capture Private constraints (LKG policy, no hard dependency on an always-on central service).

Allison Grant: I’ll draft the metrics/events list and drop it in the ADR comments.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll note the subject propagation expectations + how we represent scope in the request context.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: thanks all

title: #eng-platform: 1739393318
preview:
Ethan Park: circling back on the version compatibility policy for Private upgrades. we need to decide where the matrix lives + how it's kept current. also where we enforce (installer vs control plane vs terraform).

Quentin Harris: +1. i can own the matrix but we need a home w/ PR review + release process. right now it’s scattered between docs + tribal knowledge.

Benji Okafor: proposing: matrix lives in main `redwood` repo (not installer), so it’s tied to control plane releases. installer consumes it (pinned by target version) and blocks unsupported paths.

Connor O'Brien: i like it in `redwood` repo for release-train reasons. but installer is what operators run. we need to guarantee the installer has the policy for air-gapped too.

Quentin Harris: we can do both: canonical source in `redwood` repo, and publish it as an artifact alongside Private release bundle.
- canonical: `redwood/private/compatibility/matrix.json` (or yaml)
- release pipeline: renders/snapshots + signs it into the private upgrade bundle manifest
- installer: reads local bundle copy (air-gapped) or fetches from artifact endpoint (VPC)

Sahana Iyer: where do we want enforcement? prechecks should catch, but also chart upgrades can happen outside installer sometimes (some customers…). do we enforce server-side too?

Ethan Park: yes. control plane should reject if it sees an unsupported CP<->DP combo trying to register.

Benji Okafor: enforcement points i’m thinking:
1) installer preflight (block) for unsupported upgrade path (N-2 -> N, etc)
2) control plane API gate when a DP reports version / tries to join
3) terraform validation (warn or block?) when pinning versions
4) helm chart: minimal, but can emit a warning event / fail pre-upgrade job if obvious mismatch

Rafael Mendes: SRE POV: we need deterministic behavior. ideally hard block in installer + CP. warnings in terraform are fine but don’t want “it applied anyway” surprise during incident.

Connor O'Brien: agree. terraform can be best-effort because it can’t see current cluster state. but it CAN stop folks from pinning an unsupported target version in the module.

Quentin Harris: on format: strongly prefer machine-readable + diff-friendly. yaml is nice, but json avoids parser drift across languages.
example fields:
- `private_platform_version` (aka CP)
- `installer_min_version`
- `helm_chart_version`
- `supported_upgrade_from`: list of previous private_platform_versions
- `dp_min_version` / `dp_max_version` or explicit allowed pairs
- `breaking_changes`: text for operator messaging

Allison Grant: pls add fields for audit log context. if precheck blocks, we should log “compatibility_policy_version” + “matrix_digest”. makes compliance reviews way easier.

Marcus Lin: +1 on signing. if installer fetches policy over network, we need signature verification (esp. for private artifact mirrors). if it’s in the bundle, integrity should be tied to bundle signature.

Vivek Kulkarni: product angle: we should keep the promise simple: default supported path is N-1 -> N for GA. matrix should allow more, but only when we explicitly bless it.

Ethan Park: question: who updates the matrix when we cut a release? release eng or platform?

Connor O'Brien: release eng will enforce that it *exists*, but owner should update content. best: PR template checkbox + CI gate.
- when `redwood` release tag is cut, CI checks that matrix has entry for that version
- and that `supported_upgrade_from` includes previous GA version (or rationale)

Quentin Harris: works. i’ll own updating entries, but i want an explicit review requirement from SRE + release eng for changes that expand supported paths.

Rafael Mendes: can do. treat “supported paths” like an API contract. require @eng-sre approval if we add N-2 -> N support or widen ranges.

Sahana Iyer: CI coverage: can we add a test that runs `compat eval` across the matrix to ensure no impossible transitions? (like pointing to non-existent versions, or making a version upgradeable from itself only).

Benji Okafor: yeah, we can build a tiny validator.
- validates DAG has no cycles (or at least no weird ones)
- ensures each entry has `installer_min_version`
- ensures semantic version parseable
- ensures `supported_upgrade_from` references known versions

Allison Grant: and please ensure we emit a single stable “policy eval result” object in logs. support bundles should include:
- matrix file used
- digest
- evaluation output (allowed/blocked + reason)

Ethan Park: where should the evaluator live? i’d rather not duplicate logic in installer + CP.

Benji Okafor: make a small library in `redwood` repo (go?) and vendor it into installer? or publish it as a WASM? (kidding… mostly)

Connor O'Brien: keep it boring. matrix lives in `redwood` repo. evaluator logic can exist in two places but must share test vectors.
- put “golden tests” in `redwood/private/compatibility/testdata`
- installer + CP both run those vectors in CI

Quentin Harris: that’s fine. i’ll provide the test vectors.

Marcus Lin: also consider downgrade/rollback semantics. do we need matrix entries for rollback targets? i.e. if upgrade to N fails mid-flight, rollback to N-1 must be allowed even if forward path is blocked.

Ethan Park: good call. we should allow rollback to the “from” version if upgrade plan was generated for that path. matrix can include `rollback_allowed_to` implicitly = supported_upgrade_from. installer can store the “from” version in upgrade state.

Rafael Mendes: + add messaging: if blocked, remediation should point to “install newer installer” vs “unsupported jump”. operator shouldn’t have to guess.

Quentin Harris: i’ll add `block_reason_codes` mapping + suggested action text. e.g.
- `UNSUPPORTED_PATH` -> “Only N-1 -> N supported. Upgrade to X first.”
- `INSTALLER_TOO_OLD` -> “Upgrade installer to >= a.b.c”

Connor O'Brien: action items:
- Quentin: draft matrix format + initial location PR
- Benji/Ethan: decide enforcement hooks (installer precheck + CP gate)
- Connor: add release gating check in pipeline

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: i’ll start a doc snippet for “enforcement points” so we can align with installer PR.

Quentin Harris: i’ll drop a PR early with just skeleton + validator so we can bikeshed less later.

Allison Grant: reaction from me: :eyes: on audit fields. ping me on schema.

Rafael Mendes: :+1: once we have this, SRE can tie alerts to blocked upgrades (should reduce weird cases in on-call).

Connor O'Brien: cool. let’s aim to have first version in place before next private release cut. (even if it only encodes N-1 -> N initially)

title: #eng-security: 1739319001
preview:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off the naming convention thread. We need something consistent before we lock the permission registry + start annotating endpoints. Current state is a mess (some `org.admin`, some `manage_keys`, some hardcoded strings in gateway).

Marcus Lin: Proposals I’ve heard:
- `org.api_keys.rotate`
- `controlplane.api_keys.rotate`
- `redwood.org.api_keys.rotate`
- `rbac:org:api_keys:rotate` (pls no)

Marcus Lin: Main questions:
1) separator (`.` vs `:`)
2) namespacing (product/service vs domain)
3) versioning (in string vs metadata)
4) verb set (create/update/delete/manage?)

Jordan Lee: +1. From policy engine side I strongly prefer `.` separated, lowercase, deterministic segments. Easy to lint + match prefixes.

Jordan Lee: I’d vote for: `<domain>.<resource>.<action>` where domain = customer-facing conceptual area (org/project/deployment/audit/etc), NOT internal service name.

Jordan Lee: Examples:
```
org.users.invite
org.roles.update
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
audit_logs.export
```

Naomi Feldman: PM angle: agree on conceptual domain. If we namespace by service (`gateway.*`, `controlplane.*`) it’s going to leak internals into docs + customer configs.

Priya Natarajan: +1 on not coupling to services. But we do need to avoid collisions as scope expands (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). “deployment” vs “cluster” vs “environment” will matter.

Amara Diallo: Gateway needs something that can be attached as an annotation + checked quickly. Dots are fine. Please don’t do colons; it interacts weirdly with a couple of our log parsers and header formats.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For SSO/group mapping (esp Private) customers will see these strings in some capacity (Console permission viewer, docs). Readability matters.

Ethan Park: One pain point: some actions are “read” vs “list” vs “get”. Can we standardize verbs? If we have both `read` and `get` floating around it’ll explode.

Jordan Lee: Verbs: I’d propose a constrained set:
- `get` (single)
- `list`
- `create`
- `update`
- `delete`
- `rotate` (special case)
- `enable` / `disable` (special)
- `export` (audit/log export)

Jordan Lee: And avoid `manage` unless it’s truly composite. `manage` becomes a permission bucket and breaks least privilege.

Marcus Lin: Agree. We should push people away from `manage_*` unless we’re intentionally creating a coarse role template.

Naomi Feldman: What about `view` vs `read`? Some UX language says “View audit logs”. But for the permission string I’m fine with `get/list`.

Amara Diallo: We should align with API patterns: HTTP GET on `/audit-logs` maps to `audit_logs.list`, GET on `/audit-logs/{id}` maps to `audit_logs.get`. Makes it easy in codegen.

Priya Natarajan: +1. That gives us a clean mapping rule.

Brian Murphy: Jumping in — docs team will thank you if we do `get/list/create/update/delete` and avoid synonyms.

Marcus Lin: Versioning question: do we ever put `v1` in the permission string? e.g. `org.api_keys.rotate.v1`?

Jordan Lee: Please no. If permissions are part of customer contracts, changing the string is painful. Better: permission ID is stable, and we add metadata:
- `introduced_at`
- `deprecated_at`
- `replaced_by`

Jordan Lee: Then policy bundle tooling can emit warnings if you still use deprecated perms.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: If we don’t version in the string, how do we handle semantics changes? Like splitting one permission into two.

Jordan Lee: That’s a migration problem, not a naming problem. We keep old perm working (maybe mapped internally) while new perms exist. Eventually deprecate + remove with a long runway.

Naomi Feldman: I like that. For enterprise questionnaires, we can say “permissions are stable identifiers, with explicit deprecation policy”.

Priya Natarajan: +1 but we need a hard rule: never change semantics of an existing permission. If semantics change, create a new permission and deprecate the old.

Marcus Lin: That’s basically what ADR-014 should state.

Amara Diallo: From gateway perspective, having stable strings helps caching too. If we embed version in the string we’ll end up with multiple checks and weird dual-read behavior.

Ethan Park: Namespacing question: do we want `org.*` and `project.*` etc. Or `console.*`? Some console-only actions (like “view billing page”) shouldn’t be permissions at all IMO.

Naomi Feldman: Console page access should be derived from API permissions, not new console-only permissions. Otherwise we create UI drift.

Marcus Lin: +1. “Sensitive actions” should map to actual state changes / data access. UI routes should call APIs and get denied there.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For Private installer/bootstrap we might need a couple of “platform install” actions that aren’t customer API actions.

Priya Natarajan: That’s still a domain though. Maybe `platform.install.bootstrap` or `private_deployments.*`?

Jordan Lee: We can use a `platform.*` domain for operator-only actions. Keep it minimal and clearly documented as operator scope.

Marcus Lin: Ok. Another naming nit: pluralization and underscores. `api_keys` vs `apikeys` vs `api-keys`.

Jordan Lee: Use snake_case for multiword segments (`api_keys`, `audit_logs`, `routing_policies`). All lowercase. No hyphens.

Amara Diallo: +1. Hyphens are annoying in some tooling. Snake_case is easiest.

Naomi Feldman: So proposed format:
`<domain>.<resource>.<action>`
- `domain`: `org`, `project`, `deployment`, `audit_logs`, `billing`, `platform` (?)
- `resource`: snake_case
- `action`: from allowed verbs

Naomi Feldman: Small question: is `audit_logs` a domain or resource? Example `audit_logs.export` has only 2 segments.

Jordan Lee: Good catch. I’d rather keep 3 segments always. So domain `audit`, resource `logs`, action `export` -> `audit.logs.export`.

Marcus Lin: That reads weird but is consistent.

Ethan Park: Alternative: treat “audit_logs” as resource and have domain `org` or `project`? Like `org.audit_logs.export`.

Priya Natarajan: I like `org.audit_logs.export` because audit logs are scoped to org anyway (at least today). And we might have `project.audit_logs.export` later if we add project-level audit streams.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. Also makes scoping clearer in docs (“This role can export org audit logs”).

Jordan Lee: Works for me. Then domain is the scope container, not the service.

Jordan Lee: So:
```
org.audit_logs.list
org.audit_logs.export
org.api_keys.rotate
project.routing_policies.update
deployment.model_pins.update
```

Marcus Lin: That’s pretty solid.

Amara Diallo: For routing, are we sure it’s `project.routing_policies.update` and not `project.routing.update`? “policy” is an overloaded word here.

Priya Natarajan: We should match the actual object name in the control plane: “routing policy” is the thing with fallback + region weights, so `routing_policies` is fine.

Naomi Feldman: Another question: do we include “environment” as a domain or as a scope on the resource? Like `project.environments.update` vs `environment.routing_policies.update`.

Jordan Lee: Naming doesn’t encode scope. Scope is in the evaluation input (org_id/project_id/env_id/cluster_id). Permission string should not vary based on scope.

Jordan Lee: So you can have `project.routing_policies.update` evaluated at env scope depending on request context. (Policy engine checks binding scope).

Marcus Lin: Right, scope semantics are separate.

Brian Murphy: For docs, we should include a “permission naming rules” section + a “how to request a new permission” template. Otherwise every team will invent new patterns.

Marcus Lin: Action items:
- Jordan: update ADR-014 draft with:
  - dot-separated, lowercase, snake_case segments
  - no service names in permission IDs
  - no version suffix in string; versioning via metadata + deprecation policy
  - verb set + guidance against `manage`
- Naomi: sanity-check against customer-facing terminology + console UX (permission viewer/search)
- Amara/Ethan: confirm gateway + control plane annotations can support `org.*` / `project.*` patterns cleanly

Priya Natarajan: +1. Also add lint rules in the permission registry PR so we fail CI on format violations.

Jordan Lee: On it. I’ll drop a PR comment in `pr-28134` once I’ve updated the linter rules.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: Also please reserve `internal.*` or `platform.*` now, so we don’t end up with random “ops_*” strings later.

Marcus Lin: Good call. Let’s reserve `platform.*` for operator-only actions.

Naomi Feldman: Works. One more: should we prefix everything with `redwood.`?

Jordan Lee: I don’t think so. Adds noise, no extra value. These aren’t globally unique identifiers; they’re scoped to Redwood policy bundle.

Priya Natarajan: Agree. Keep short.

Marcus Lin: Consensus seems: no `redwood.` prefix.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. In UI the shorter the better.

Marcus Lin: Cool. I’ll mark this thread as the input to ADR-014 and link it in Confluence. Thanks all.

Jordan Lee: I don’t think so. Adds noise, no extra value. These aren’t globally unique identifiers; they’re scoped to Redwood policy bundle.

Priya Natarajan: Agree. Keep short.

Marcus Lin: Consensus seems: no `redwood.` prefix.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. In UI the shorter the better.

Marcus Lin: Cool. I’ll mark this thread as the input to ADR-014 and link it in Confluence. Thanks all.

title: #eng-releases: 1739233033
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Starting a thread to lock the GA rollout plan for Route Experiments (region-by-region + account tier). Need: (1) order, (2) monitoring checkpoints, (3) rollback criteria + who flips what. Goal is to have something we can paste into the 2025.04 release train notes.

Talia Benson: +1. Also please call out what “GA” means in practice (still behind kill switch, still soft-stop by default, etc). Support will ask.

Marissa Cole: Product side: GA scope = create/start/stop experiments in Console, results dashboard w/ deltas + guardrail banners, audit trail, RBAC gates. Non-goals still hold (no full stat-sig engine; no custom metrics ingestion).

Dylan Brooks: From Console: we can ship UI on by default *but* still hide for plans that don’t have entitlement. Also want a staged enablement because of results query load (we saw spikes in staging during the beta dashboards).

Chloe Martin: Backend: APIs are stable; we’re relying on rollup tables for results. Biggest GA risk is “results stale” if aggregation lags or attribution drops.

Sean Gallagher: SRE request: GA must mean “progressive enablement” not “flip everything globally”. We need regional controls and clear rollback triggers.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed rollout units (tell me if missing):
- UI / nav: `console.route_experiments_ui`
- Control plane mutations: `experiments.ga_enabled` (or reuse `experiments.beta_enabled` but change semantics)
- Data plane evaluator: `routing.route_experiments_enabled`
- Global kill switch: `routing.route_experiments_kill_switch`
- (Optional) hard-stop guardrails: `experiments.guardrail_hard_stop_enabled` stays OFF by default

Jada Williams: +1 on those. Re semantics: I’d prefer we keep `experiments.beta_enabled` as-is and add `experiments.ga_enabled` so we can separate “old beta allowlist” from “GA entitlement + progressive rollout”.

Jordan Lee: Agree. Also: regional scoping has to be consistent across control plane + routing. If we do “enable in us-east only”, start should validate region eligibility so customers don’t create experiments and then wonder why traffic isn’t splitting elsewhere.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC/entitlements note: GA rollout should not bypass plan checks. Even if flags are on, non-entitled tenants should get “feature not available” in UI and 403 on mutation endpoints.

Marcus Lin: Security/compliance: for enterprise accounts, rollout must not change audit behavior. If we enable feature for more tenants, audit export volume will increase; ensure retention/export is stable and no PII leaks in change diffs.

Logan Wright: Telemetry: we should treat “attribution drop rate” as a GA gating metric. If the experiment_id/variant_id tags aren’t present in enough requests, results are garbage and customers will lose trust.

Allison Grant: Dashboards/alerts: US regions are good. eu-west has the updated attribution drop panel and propagation lag panel now, but I still want a slower ramp there given last month’s skew + missing sticky key incident.

Connor O'Brien: Ok, here’s a concrete phased plan. Please react / edit.

Connor O'Brien: Phase A (Day 0): internal + design partners only
- Regions: us-east-1 only
- Accounts: Redwood internal dogfood org(s) + Acme AI + Northbridge (only if pinned to us-east)
- Enable flags: UI + ga_enabled + routing.route_experiments_enabled (region-scoped)
- Duration: 24h
- Checkpoints (must be green):
  1) Attribution drop rate < 0.5% (15m window)
  2) Config propagation lag p95 < 60s, p99 < 180s
  3) Router error rate no worse than baseline +0.1%
  4) Results rollup freshness: median < 20m, p95 < 45m
  5) DB load from results API within budget (Chloe/Dylan to provide query cost threshold)

Rafael Mendes: Add a checkpoint for “metrics cardinality / spend”. We got burned once when tags exploded.

Logan Wright: +1. We can use “otel span tag cardinality guard” metric + cost anomaly alert. Suggest: “metrics pipeline lag < 2m” and “no cardinality limiter drops > 0.1%”.

Connor O'Brien: Noted, adding as (6) and (7).

Connor O'Brien: Phase B (Day 1-2): expand regions for same limited accounts
- Regions: us-west-2 next
- Accounts: same list, but ensure Northbridge is pinned to us-west-2 before we claim enabled
- Duration: 24h
- Same checkpoints + additionally: compare split accuracy distribution (variant allocation) for each region (skew detection)

Jordan Lee: For skew detection: we should explicitly gate on “% of traffic with missing stickiness key”. If it’s high, splits get noisy and customers blame us. Suggest threshold: missing sticky key < 2% for the route (or show warning / block Start).

Dylan Brooks: Console already blocks Start unless they acknowledge missing stickiness key risk; but we can still track it as rollout health.

Connor O'Brien: Great, we’ll add “missing sticky key rate < 2%” as a checkpoint for Phase B+.

Connor O'Brien: Phase C (Day 3-5): limited GA to paid tiers (Pro + Enterprise) in US only
- Regions: us-east-1 + us-west-2
- Accounts: all Pro + Enterprise tenants (hosted + dedicated control planes), excluding Private/on-prem packages until week 2
- Ramp: 10% of eligible tenants -> 50% -> 100% (each step min 24h)
- Monitoring: same checkpoints, plus support ticket rate watch (results stale / headers confusion)

Talia Benson: Can we add a “support readiness checkpoint” between 10% and 50%? Like: docs live, macro ready, oncall runbook link in Console banner.

Marissa Cole: Yes. Docs PR is almost ready; we should not go to 50% without it.

Connor O'Brien: Added: “Docs + support macros live” gate before 50%.

Connor O'Brien: Phase D (Week 2): EU region + remaining tiers
- Regions: eu-west-1
- Ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 100% for Pro+Enterprise
- Then: consider enabling for eligible “Growth” tier (if pricing says so)

Allison Grant: For eu-west, I’d prefer starting at 1% (not 5%) and only during staffed hours. Also need a clear rollback if propagation lag spikes; EU had more cache inconsistency historically.

Sean Gallagher: +1. EU rollout should be daytime-only and have a dedicated oncall watch.

Connor O'Brien: Ok EU starts at 1% during business hours UTC, staffed watch.

Chloe Martin: Re “query cost threshold”: we can set an alert on results API p95 latency + DB CPU. Proposed rollback if DB CPU > 75% for >30m attributable to results queries.

Sean Gallagher: Rollback criteria should be explicit + actionable (flip which flag). Suggest:
- Sev1/Sev2 linked to route experiments -> flip kill switch globally
- Region-specific incident -> disable `routing.route_experiments_enabled` in that region + leave UI read-only
- Data quality incident (attribution drop) -> disable results dashboard (if we have a UI flag) OR disable routing

Dylan Brooks: We do have `console.route_experiments_results_enabled` as a separate gate (we added it after the staging DB spike). We can use it to keep creation/management available but hide results charts if rollups are broken.

Connor O'Brien: Perfect. Adding to controls list:
- `console.route_experiments_results_enabled` (UI) — can be disabled independently to protect DB / hide stale data.

Rafael Mendes: Rollback triggers (suggested thresholds):
- Attribution drop rate > 2% for 30m (any region) => stop ramp, investigate; if > 5% => disable routing flag in that region
- Propagation lag p95 > 5m for 30m => disable routing in that region (prevents inconsistent assignment)
- p99 latency regression > 10% vs baseline for 30m => disable routing in that region
- Error rate regression > 0.5% absolute for 15m => kill switch global if widespread

Logan Wright: +1. Also: if we see cardinality limiter drops > 1% (15m) or metrics pipeline lag > 10m, results become misleading. That should disable results UI flag and pause ramp.

Marcus Lin: For audit: if audit export pipeline backs up (delivery latency > SLA), that’s a compliance issue for enterprise. Add a checkpoint/rollback for that too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: And any RBAC bypass bug (non-admin can start/stop) should be immediate kill switch + incident.

Connor O'Brien: Summarizing rollback matrix (please sanity check):
- Global kill: `routing.route_experiments_kill_switch` (SRE + Release only)
- Per-region stop: set `routing.route_experiments_enabled` OFF in region
- Data quality / DB protection: set `console.route_experiments_results_enabled` OFF
- Control plane stop (prevent new experiments): `experiments.ga_enabled` OFF

Sean Gallagher: Works. Also: confirm who has perms to flip these in prod. Needs to be limited and audited.

Connor O'Brien: Current plan: Release Managers + SRE oncall can flip prod flags. Changes logged in FeatureFlagService. Kaitlyn/Marcus ok?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes as long as roles are enforced and we have break-glass documented.

Marcus Lin: Ok, but please ensure “flag change events” get sent to audit export too (at least for enterprise tenants) or to a security log channel.

Allison Grant: One more operational request: add a Slack announcement automation when we move ramp steps (10%->50%->100%), including dashboards links + rollback instructions.

Connor O'Brien: I can do manual posts in #eng-releases + #eng-sre for Phase C/D. We can automate later.

Release Bot: Heads up: release train milestone reminder — 2025.04 GA candidate features need final rollout plan doc link by EOD tomorrow.

Talia Benson: Connor can you paste the final plan into the GA checklist ticket + link it here? Also include the “staffed hours” EU note.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Next actions:
- Connor: write 1-page GA rollout plan (Phases A-D + rollback matrix) and link in release notes + PM ticket
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm results UI flag and DB thresholds
- Logan/Allison: confirm alert queries + dashboard links per region
- Sean/Rafael: confirm oncall watch schedule for EU ramp

Sean Gallagher: Good. Also please schedule a quick 30m “ramp gameday” before Phase C (simulate disable results UI, disable routing, flip kill switch).

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: I’ll send a calendar for Friday.

Marissa Cole: Thanks all. As long as we keep hard-stop OFF by default and we message results freshness expectations (15-20m), I’m comfortable calling this GA.

Dylan Brooks: +1. Also adding tooltip copy re headers confusion (x-redwood-variant-id) before Phase C.

Connor O'Brien: Closing loop once doc is posted.

title: #eng-platform: 1739305111
preview:
Logan Wright: quick review request on PR feedback for checklist progress API perms + audit logging. PR-28431 is getting a lot of :eyes: from security + support. Can we align on the model today so Chloe/Jada can push updates?

github-bot: PR #28431 “add-checklist-progress-api” updated
- requested review: @loganw @chloe.m @marcus.lin
- latest commit: `f6a1c4d` “wire authz + add audit_log skeleton”

Chloe Martin: yep. main open questions from comments:
1) should Console call `GET /v1/onboarding/checklist/state` as end-user or as console-service?
2) do we allow *any* write endpoint besides the internal event ingest?
3) audit logging: what’s minimum required fields + where does it land

Jada Williams: +1 and there’s also a comment about us accepting `tenant_id` in the request body for the “mark step complete” endpoint (draft). reviewer said that’s a footgun.

Dev Patel: from Support side: we need the override but it has to be safe + auditable. We’ve had a bunch of internal requests like “customer says they did step X but state is stuck”, and we end up escalating to eng.

Marcus Lin: adding security lens: principle is “derived tenant from auth, never caller-provided”, and “human overrides require explicit reason + immutable audit trail”. Also: keep payload PII-free.

Logan Wright: ok, proposing:
- read state: Console backend calls as console-service (service-to-service) *but* scoped to the end-user’s tenant based on their session. (So the browser never talks to progress-service directly.)
- write events: only `POST /internal/v1/onboarding/events` from gateway/control plane w/ mTLS + service auth.
- support override: separate endpoint `POST /v1/onboarding/checklist/admin/override` behind `support_ops` role. no customer tokens.

Nadia Rahman: re: read state, can we do it as end-user JWT? would be nice but we’d then need to expose progress-service publicly + deal with CORS, WAF rules, etc. I’d rather keep it behind console backend.

Dylan Brooks: +1 keep it behind console backend. also lets us evolve without breaking the browser clients.

Chloe Martin: ack. so for the console-service call: we still need to ensure console can’t fetch arbitrary tenants. easiest is require `X-RW-Tenant-Id` header from console, but then validate it against the authenticated console session claims.

Jada Williams: right now in PR it’s `tenant_id` query param. I can change to “no tenant_id param; tenant resolved by auth middleware” for all non-admin endpoints.

Marcus Lin: strongly prefer “no tenant_id param” where possible.
- if you *must* pass tenant, make it an internal header and verify it server-side.
- public APIs: tenant must come from token claims.

Logan Wright: for non-admin read endpoint, we can have:
- Console sends `Authorization: console-service` (service token)
- plus `X-RW-Actor-User-Id` + `X-RW-Actor-Tenant-Id` coming from console after validating session
- progress-service enforces: actor tenant id is required and becomes the tenant scope. no ability to query others.

Chloe Martin: that works. We already have that pattern for a couple internal services.

Dev Patel: admin override: can it be limited to internal staff only + require a reason string? we’ll use that in support notes.

Jada Williams: current draft has `reason` optional. I can make it required + min length.

Marcus Lin: yes, make it required. also include “ticket reference” field (Jira/Slack link). optional is ok but helpful.

Nadia Rahman: audit logging: where do we want it? we have the standard audit log pipeline for console/admin actions. should we send to that or write to a local DB table?

Logan Wright: minimum viable:
- append-only table `onboarding_checklist_audit_log`
- also emit to our audit log stream (same sink as other admin actions) for retention/search
If only one for MVP, I’d pick stream (so Support can find it) + store request_id in DB.

Chloe Martin: constraints: emitting to stream is easy, but we still want local for debugging + unit tests. I’ll do both but keep DB minimal.

Jada Williams: what fields are required? PR comment asked for “who/what changed state” + “before/after”.

Marcus Lin: required for human override:
- ts
- actor_type (support_user | system)
- actor_id (internal user id) and actor_email optional (if that’s allowed; email is borderline PII but internal)
- tenant_id
- action (override_mark_complete, override_reset, override_note)
- step_id
- from_status -> to_status
- reason
- source_ip + user_agent (optional but useful)
- request_id / trace_id
For system event ingestion we can log less, but should still have trace_id + dedupe key.

Nadia Rahman: for system writes, we should log:
- event_name
- producer_service
- idempotency_key
- tenant_id
- step_id
- resulting transition
(no payload)

Logan Wright: agree. and importantly: do not log prompt text, api keys, model inputs, etc.

Dylan Brooks: one more: permissions for “admin override” endpoint. We need to define `support_ops` vs `console_admin`.

Dev Patel: support leadership asked for “Support + CS” eventually. for MVP I’m ok with Support only. If we can gate on an allowlist group, even better.

Chloe Martin: I can wire it to our internal RBAC groups: `support_oncall` and `support_leads`. We’ll start with `support_leads` only to reduce blast radius.

Logan Wright: sounds good. also add a rate limit on admin override (per actor) so it can’t be spammed.

Jada Williams: PR feedback also asked for a “dry run” mode. do we need that?

Dev Patel: not necessary. we just need a “note” field and audit log.

Marcus Lin: +1 no dry run.

Nadia Rahman: last detail: should admin override allow setting arbitrary state or only monotonic transitions?

Chloe Martin: my instinct:
- allow `mark_complete` (incomplete->complete)
- allow `reset` (complete->incomplete) but only with reason + ticket ref
- do not allow setting “in_progress”/intermediate states. keep state machine small.

Logan Wright: perfect. that matches the race-condition fix we did elsewhere (monotonic for system, explicit reset for humans).

Jada Williams: ok action items for PR-28431 update:
- remove tenant_id query/body from non-admin endpoints; derive scope from auth middleware
- add admin override endpoint behind RBAC group `support_leads`
- make `reason` required; add optional `ticket_ref`
- implement append-only audit log row + emit audit event (no payload)
- add unit tests for authz + audit emission

Marcus Lin: please tag me on the updated diff; I’ll do a quick privacy check once you land it.

Dev Patel: also loop me in when override is ready; we’ll draft the Support macro with “what reason to enter + where to paste ticket link”.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: thanks all. Jada/Chloe ping when updated; I’ll resolve the PR comments + we can unblock merge.

github-bot: Chloe Martin pushed 3 commits to PR #28431
- `1c2b9d0` “derive tenant scope from auth; remove tenant_id params”
- `7a0e4aa` “add admin override (support_leads) + required reason”
- `d13fe21` “emit audit events + append-only audit table”

Chloe Martin: updates are in. remaining TODO is rate limit on override (I can do next PR if it’s too much churn now).

Logan Wright: next PR is fine. I’ll approve w/ note. Marcus can you take another look?

Marcus Lin: looking now.

Marcus Lin: reviewed. audit fields look good. small ask: ensure `ticket_ref` is length-limited + validated to avoid someone pasting a full customer payload by mistake.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll cap at 256 chars and strip newlines.

Dev Patel: works for us.

Logan Wright: once that’s in, we’re good to merge.

github-bot: PR #28431 merged into `main`
