Jada Williams: +1 and there’s also a comment about us accepting `tenant_id` in the request body for the “mark step complete” endpoint (draft). reviewer said that’s a footgun.

Dev Patel: from Support side: we need the override but it has to be safe + auditable. We’ve had a bunch of internal requests like “customer says they did step X but state is stuck”, and we end up escalating to eng.

Marcus Lin: adding security lens: principle is “derived tenant from auth, never caller-provided”, and “human overrides require explicit reason + immutable audit trail”. Also: keep payload PII-free.

Logan Wright: ok, proposing:
- read state: Console backend calls as console-service (service-to-service) *but* scoped to the end-user’s tenant based on their session. (So the browser never talks to progress-service directly.)
- write events: only `POST /internal/v1/onboarding/events` from gateway/control plane w/ mTLS + service auth.
- support override: separate endpoint `POST /v1/onboarding/checklist/admin/override` behind `support_ops` role. no customer tokens.

Nadia Rahman: re: read state, can we do it as end-user JWT? would be nice but we’d then need to expose progress-service publicly + deal with CORS, WAF rules, etc. I’d rather keep it behind console backend.

Dylan Brooks: +1 keep it behind console backend. also lets us evolve without breaking the browser clients.

Chloe Martin: ack. so for the console-service call: we still need to ensure console can’t fetch arbitrary tenants. easiest is require `X-RW-Tenant-Id` header from console, but then validate it against the authenticated console session claims.

Jada Williams: right now in PR it’s `tenant_id` query param. I can change to “no tenant_id param; tenant resolved by auth middleware” for all non-admin endpoints.

Marcus Lin: strongly prefer “no tenant_id param” where possible.
- if you *must* pass tenant, make it an internal header and verify it server-side.
- public APIs: tenant must come from token claims.

Logan Wright: for non-admin read endpoint, we can have:
- Console sends `Authorization: console-service` (service token)
- plus `X-RW-Actor-User-Id` + `X-RW-Actor-Tenant-Id` coming from console after validating session
- progress-service enforces: actor tenant id is required and becomes the tenant scope. no ability to query others.

Chloe Martin: that works. We already have that pattern for a couple internal services.

Dev Patel: admin override: can it be limited to internal staff only + require a reason string? we’ll use that in support notes.

Jada Williams: current draft has `reason` optional. I can make it required + min length.

Marcus Lin: yes, make it required. also include “ticket reference” field (Jira/Slack link). optional is ok but helpful.

Nadia Rahman: audit logging: where do we want it? we have the standard audit log pipeline for console/admin actions. should we send to that or write to a local DB table?

Logan Wright: minimum viable:
- append-only table `onboarding_checklist_audit_log`
- also emit to our audit log stream (same sink as other admin actions) for retention/search
If only one for MVP, I’d pick stream (so Support can find it) + store request_id in DB.

Chloe Martin: constraints: emitting to stream is easy, but we still want local for debugging + unit tests. I’ll do both but keep DB minimal.

Jada Williams: what fields are required? PR comment asked for “who/what changed state” + “before/after”.

Marcus Lin: required for human override:
- ts
- actor_type (support_user | system)
- actor_id (internal user id) and actor_email optional (if that’s allowed; email is borderline PII but internal)
- tenant_id
- action (override_mark_complete, override_reset, override_note)
- step_id
- from_status -> to_status
- reason
- source_ip + user_agent (optional but useful)
- request_id / trace_id
For system event ingestion we can log less, but should still have trace_id + dedupe key.

Nadia Rahman: for system writes, we should log:
- event_name
- producer_service
- idempotency_key
- tenant_id
- step_id
- resulting transition
(no payload)

Logan Wright: agree. and importantly: do not log prompt text, api keys, model inputs, etc.

Dylan Brooks: one more: permissions for “admin override” endpoint. We need to define `support_ops` vs `console_admin`.

Dev Patel: support leadership asked for “Support + CS” eventually. for MVP I’m ok with Support only. If we can gate on an allowlist group, even better.

Chloe Martin: I can wire it to our internal RBAC groups: `support_oncall` and `support_leads`. We’ll start with `support_leads` only to reduce blast radius.

Logan Wright: sounds good. also add a rate limit on admin override (per actor) so it can’t be spammed.

Jada Williams: PR feedback also asked for a “dry run” mode. do we need that?

Dev Patel: not necessary. we just need a “note” field and audit log.

Marcus Lin: +1 no dry run.

Nadia Rahman: last detail: should admin override allow setting arbitrary state or only monotonic transitions?

Chloe Martin: my instinct:
- allow `mark_complete` (incomplete->complete)
- allow `reset` (complete->incomplete) but only with reason + ticket ref
- do not allow setting “in_progress”/intermediate states. keep state machine small.

Logan Wright: perfect. that matches the race-condition fix we did elsewhere (monotonic for system, explicit reset for humans).

Jada Williams: ok action items for PR-28431 update:
- remove tenant_id query/body from non-admin endpoints; derive scope from auth middleware
- add admin override endpoint behind RBAC group `support_leads`
- make `reason` required; add optional `ticket_ref`
- implement append-only audit log row + emit audit event (no payload)
- add unit tests for authz + audit emission

Marcus Lin: please tag me on the updated diff; I’ll do a quick privacy check once you land it.

Dev Patel: also loop me in when override is ready; we’ll draft the Support macro with “what reason to enter + where to paste ticket link”.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: thanks all. Jada/Chloe ping when updated; I’ll resolve the PR comments + we can unblock merge.

github-bot: Chloe Martin pushed 3 commits to PR #28431
- `1c2b9d0` “derive tenant scope from auth; remove tenant_id params”
- `7a0e4aa` “add admin override (support_leads) + required reason”
- `d13fe21` “emit audit events + append-only audit table”

Chloe Martin: updates are in. remaining TODO is rate limit on override (I can do next PR if it’s too much churn now).

Logan Wright: next PR is fine. I’ll approve w/ note. Marcus can you take another look?

Marcus Lin: looking now.

Marcus Lin: reviewed. audit fields look good. small ask: ensure `ticket_ref` is length-limited + validated to avoid someone pasting a full customer payload by mistake.

Jada Williams: good call. I’ll cap at 256 chars and strip newlines.

Dev Patel: works for us.

Logan Wright: once that’s in, we’re good to merge.

github-bot: PR #28431 merged into `main`

title: #docs: 1739294021
preview:
Kira Thompson: need to lock the customer-facing FAQ for Private backup/restore today so we can merge docs PR. can folks review these 3 areas: (1) what’s backed up, (2) how customers should test restores, (3) support boundaries

Kira Thompson: draft FAQ section here (wip): https://github.com/redwood/redwood-docs/pull/812#discussion_r_FAQ

Elliot Price: skimmed. overall good. a couple questions read too “internal runbook-y”. we should keep it crisp + avoid implying we back up the entire k8s cluster by default.

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. biggest customer confusion: “is this like velero?” they expect full cluster snapshots. we need to clearly say we back up Redwood control plane state + required config, not arbitrary namespaces.

Sean Gallagher: yep. and please add a line that restore testing should happen in a non-prod environment first. we’ve had folks try to “test” by restoring over prod and then open a P0 :)

Marcus Lin: from security side: make sure we’re explicit that we do NOT include plaintext secret material in the backup artifact (only references), unless customer explicitly configures a secret backend export on their side.

Hanae Suzuki: re secrets: we should spell out “KMS key access is required at restore time” (for envelope encryption). otherwise folks in airgapped/locked-down envs get surprised.

Kira Thompson: ok, proposing FAQ items below — pls nit and confirm wording:

Kira Thompson: Q: What does Redwood back up?
A: Redwood Private backup captures control-plane state required to reconstruct tenants/projects, routing/config policies, and platform configuration that drives serving behavior. It also captures the deployment’s release/config values needed for deterministic redeploy.

Kira Thompson: Q: What is NOT included?
A: Data-plane runtime state (pods are treated as stateless), customer application data, and model artifacts stored outside the Redwood control plane. Kubernetes cluster-level state (e.g., full etcd) is not automatically backed up by Redwood.

Kira Thompson: Q: Are secrets included?
A: Backup artifacts are encrypted, but Redwood does not export plaintext secrets by default. Backups include references/metadata to external secret stores (KMS/Secrets Manager/Vault) and require those systems + keys to be available during restore.

Kira Thompson: Q: How do I test restores?
A: Run a periodic restore drill into a staging cluster using the same backup storage backend and key material, then run the validation checklist (health checks + API smoke tests). Do not test restores by overwriting prod.

Kira Thompson: Q: What help can Redwood Support provide?
A: Support can provide guidance, review logs/validation reports, and help interpret errors, but cannot access customer infrastructure or run commands in customer environments. For hands-on assistance, engage Solutions/Professional Services.

Markus Klein: wording looks right. can we add one more in support boundary: “Support will not guarantee RPO/RTO” / “RPO/RTO depends on your backup cadence + infra” (customers keep trying to make it an SLA).

Vivek Kulkarni: yes please. also add a Q about “How often should we run backups / restore drills?” even if answer is “depends” but give recommended starting point.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we can give a safe default: daily backups + weekly/monthly restore drill depending on environment criticality. and link to “restore validation test plan” (customer-safe subset).

Elliot Price: also, for “what does Redwood back up” we should list concrete components in plain language. otherwise people assume everything.

Marcus Lin: on secrets answer: avoid “metadata” (too vague). maybe “references to secrets (names/paths), not secret values”. and explicitly call out that the KMS key must be accessible and permissions valid at restore time.

Hanae Suzuki: also call out key rotation: “older backups remain decryptable if key policy allows”. maybe too deep for FAQ, but at least mention: if you revoke key access, you can’t restore.

Kira Thompson: ok updated phrasing (proposed):

Kira Thompson: Q: What does the Redwood Private backup include?
A: The backup includes Redwood control-plane state and configuration required to rebuild the Private deployment, including:
• tenant/project configuration and routing policy state
• control-plane databases/config stores (Redwood-managed)
• the installer/Helm configuration needed to redeploy the same version (release values/manifests)
Backups are stored as encrypted artifacts in your configured backend (e.g., S3+KMS, NFS).

Kira Thompson: Q: What does it NOT include?
A: The backup does not include:
• data-plane runtime state (GPU workers are treated as stateless)
• customer application data or third-party system data
• plaintext secret values
• a full Kubernetes cluster snapshot (e.g., etcd). If you need cluster-level recovery, use your standard Kubernetes/infra tooling.

Kira Thompson: Q: Are secrets included?
A: No. Backups include references to secrets (names/paths) but not the secret values themselves. Restore requires access to your secret store and the encryption keys used to protect the backup artifact (KMS/HSM where configured).

Kira Thompson: Q: How do we test restores?
A: We recommend scheduled restore drills into a non-production cluster:
1) verify access to the backup backend + keys
2) restore into a clean staging cluster using the same Redwood version
3) run the post-restore validation checklist (health checks + API smoke tests)
Do not test restores by restoring “over” your production control plane.

Kira Thompson: Q: What are Redwood’s recommended backup/DR cadences?
A: As a starting point, run daily backups and perform restore drills at least monthly (more frequently for higher-criticality deployments). Your achievable RPO/RTO depends on backup frequency, storage performance, and your infrastructure automation.

Kira Thompson: Q: What help can Redwood Support provide?
A: Support can help you interpret errors, review logs/validation reports, and provide guidance on documented procedures. Support cannot access your environment or execute commands in customer infrastructure. Hands-on assistance is available via Solutions/Professional Services. RPO/RTO commitments are customer-owned unless explicitly contracted.

Elliot Price: this is much clearer. small nit: “installer/Helm configuration needed to redeploy” — customers may not use Helm directly (some do Terraform). maybe say “deployment configuration (Helm values / installer config)”.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also can we link to a “validation checklist” section in docs rather than “smoke tests” only? but ok for FAQ.

Marcus Lin: secrets answer looks good now. pls add one explicit sentence: “If the decryption key is unavailable (revoked policy / deleted key), restore will fail.” that’s important.

Vivek Kulkarni: support boundary line is perfect. we should keep “unless explicitly contracted” (enterprise asks).

Markus Klein: can we soften “customer-owned” -> “depends on your environment and runbooks” (less confrontational).

Kira Thompson: ack. revised support answer:
“RPO/RTO depends on your environment and operational procedures unless explicitly covered in your contract.”

Kira Thompson: adding Marcus sentence to secrets:
“If the decryption key is unavailable (e.g., access revoked or key deleted), restore will fail.”

Elliot Price: :thumbsup: ship it.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: works for me.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: 

Hanae Suzuki: :thumbsup: (and ty for adding the key availability callout)

Kira Thompson: cool, I’ll update the PR text + ping for final review. thanks all.

Kira Thompson: (also adding a short “Velero/k8s backups” note in a non-FAQ section to avoid the “full cluster snapshot” assumption)

title: #architecture: 1739224450
preview:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more alerts, then more bot usage… spiral.

Ethan Park: question: is aggregator strictly read-only? any chance it triggers state (rollout pauses, routing changes) via interactive actions?

Miles Thornton: for v2, read-only. interactive actions are “expand diff”, “refresh”, “open dashboard”, “copy cli”. no “pause rollout” buttons.

Ravi Menon: read-only is good. we *should* still treat it as production-critical. during partial outages, it must fail predictably.

Devon Price: slack constraint: interactive action needs ACK in 3s. so aggregator has to respond fast or we do the pattern: immediate ack + “loading…” + async update.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve done the async update pattern in other bots. works, but we need idempotency keys so retries don’t post duplicate cards.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata path: we can provide last N deploys per service/region w/ rollout phase + canary status. join to PR/commit is possible but not always (hotfixes).

Benji Okafor: config diff: we can return “last 10 config mutations” and/or “diff between current and T-30m snapshot”. we need strong redaction hooks before anything hits Slack.

Marcus Lin: +1. we should assume Slack is not a secure sink. even in private channels. redaction must be server-side and testable.

Kevin Osei: and audit events. every call to config diff or privileged telemetry should emit an audit record (who/what incident/thread).

Naomi Feldman: from compliance side: we’ll want (a) scope-based access, (b) immutable audit trail, (c) clear break-glass rules. also define retention.

Miles Thornton: ok, diving into feedback requested:
1) data flow & boundaries
2) caching + coalescing
3) rate limits / circuit breakers
4) failure modes under partial outage

Ravi Menon: on (1): keep diag-aggregator dumb-ish. it should not encode “business logic” like routing decisions. it should *surface* current policy + pointers.

Mina Haddad: +1. if we embed policy eval in bot, we’ll drift from actual routing engine. better: bot shows pinned model, fallback chain, and “last routing policy change” link.

Jordan Lee: we can expose a “policy snapshot” endpoint that’s already computed by the control plane (safe subset). aggregator should fetch that.

Ethan Park: please make sure “safe subset” is explicit. policy configs can contain tenant annotations we don’t want sprayed in Slack.

Benji Okafor: for config diff, we’re planning allowlist-based rendering. everything else => “[redacted]”. secrets patterns are necessary but not sufficient.

Marcus Lin: exactly. regex-based “secret detection” will miss things and false-positive. allowlist primary, pattern-based secondary.

Allison Grant: on (2) caching: propose two layers?
- L1 in aggregator (in-memory) TTL 30-60s for interactive refresh spam
- L2 shared cache (redis) TTL 2-5m for same incident/thread to smooth bursts

Logan Wright: careful with caching telemetry. folks will treat it as real-time. if it’s 5m stale we need to label “as of” time and a refresh button.

Devon Price: UI-wise, we can show “Updated: 12:31:10Z” and “(cached)” in the card.

Rafael Mendes: + a note in degraded mode: “Live queries timing out; showing last known good context from 2m ago.”

Dylan Brooks: coalescing: yes. key should include {incident_id or thread_ts, service, region, tenant?}. if key is too broad we’ll leak tenant context.

Logan Wright: + on telemetry, avoid tenant in the query unless we need it. default to service/region aggregate for “saturation” card. tenant drill-down should be link out to Console where auth is stronger.

Miles Thornton: noted. current plan is:
- Saturation card = service/region/pool-level only
- Tenant-specific metrics only via deep link w/ RBAC

Aisha Bello: on (3) rate limits: we need budgets per thread and per user action.
- hard cap per thread: e.g. 6 refreshes / 5 min
- global cap in aggregator to protect upstreams
- backoff behavior: after cap hit, show message + links only

Allison Grant: also cap label cardinality. if telemetry query svc is asked for “by model, by tenant, by route, by status_code” we’re dead.

Logan Wright: we should define a strict set of queries the bot is allowed to run. like a fixed query catalog with parameters, not arbitrary promql.

Miles Thornton: yes, query catalog approach. aggregator calls telemetry-query svc with query_id + params (service/region/window). no raw query strings.

Ethan Park: +1. easier to permission and audit.

Kevin Osei: auditing: log (a) caller identity (bot service acct), (b) human user initiating action, (c) query_id, (d) params (redacted), (e) result size, (f) latency, (g) outcome (success/timeout/circuit-open).

Marcus Lin: and for config diff, log “fields returned” count maybe, plus whether any redactions occurred.

Benji Okafor: we can do “redaction_summary: N fields redacted” but keep it in audit logs not Slack.

Ravi Menon: on (4) partial outage behavior: define priorities.
- Must show *something* within ~10s (or slack will feel broken)
- If upstream slow: show cached + deep links
- If cached missing: show “manual commands” snippet (eg `rwctl deploys --service X --region Y`)

Connor O'Brien: for deploys, if deploy metadata svc is down, we can still pull from deploy event stream? maybe last-known state in redis.

Miles Thornton: we can cache deploy context longer (10-15m) since it doesn’t change second-to-second.

Allison Grant: but stamp it. “last deploy event seen at …”

Dylan Brooks: circuit breaker suggestion:
- per-upstream breaker in aggregator
- open when p95 latency > threshold or error rate > threshold
- when open: stop calling upstream for N seconds, serve cache + links

Logan Wright: yes. and telemetry-query svc should also have its own breaker to protect the TSDB.

Rafael Mendes: also, bot should never block incident channel during sev0. if it times out, it should say so plainly (not “unknown error”).

Devon Price: copy for error states:
- “Telemetry query timed out (10s). Showing cached context from 2m ago.”
- “Config diff unavailable: missing scope `config.diff.read`. Escalate via #help (INT ticket) or break-glass if Sev0/Sev1.”

Ethan Park: careful wording on break-glass. should route to the oncall/security process, not encourage random folks.

Naomi Feldman: agree. phrasing should be “Request elevated access via documented Sev0/Sev1 procedure (link).”

Kevin Osei: and the break-glass action itself must generate a high-signal audit event (and ideally page security-oncall if used outside sev0/1).

Miles Thornton: open question: should aggregator run in same cluster as bot or separate?

Logan Wright: separate. if bot gets wedged / restarts, we don’t want it taking cache + rate limiter with it.

Dylan Brooks: + separate deploy lets us roll diag changes without touching slack app bits. but keep it small.

Allison Grant: also consider: aggregator should have low dependency footprint. avoid calling 6 internal services. keep it to 3 + cache.

Priya Natarajan: platform POV: fine to add aggregator, but we need ownership clear. SRE owns? platform owns? shared?

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).
text:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more alerts, then more bot usage… spiral.

Ethan Park: question: is aggregator strictly read-only? any chance it triggers state (rollout pauses, routing changes) via interactive actions?

Miles Thornton: for v2, read-only. interactive actions are “expand diff”, “refresh”, “open dashboard”, “copy cli”. no “pause rollout” buttons.

Ravi Menon: read-only is good. we *should* still treat it as production-critical. during partial outages, it must fail predictably.

Devon Price: slack constraint: interactive action needs ACK in 3s. so aggregator has to respond fast or we do the pattern: immediate ack + “loading…” + async update.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve done the async update pattern in other bots. works, but we need idempotency keys so retries don’t post duplicate cards.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata path: we can provide last N deploys per service/region w/ rollout phase + canary status. join to PR/commit is possible but not always (hotfixes).

Benji Okafor: config diff: we can return “last 10 config mutations” and/or “diff between current and T-30m snapshot”. we need strong redaction hooks before anything hits Slack.

Marcus Lin: +1. we should assume Slack is not a secure sink. even in private channels. redaction must be server-side and testable.

Kevin Osei: and audit events. every call to config diff or privileged telemetry should emit an audit record (who/what incident/thread).

Naomi Feldman: from compliance side: we’ll want (a) scope-based access, (b) immutable audit trail, (c) clear break-glass rules. also define retention.

Miles Thornton: ok, diving into feedback requested:
1) data flow & boundaries
2) caching + coalescing
3) rate limits / circuit breakers
4) failure modes under partial outage

Ravi Menon: on (1): keep diag-aggregator dumb-ish. it should not encode “business logic” like routing decisions. it should *surface* current policy + pointers.

Mina Haddad: +1. if we embed policy eval in bot, we’ll drift from actual routing engine. better: bot shows pinned model, fallback chain, and “last routing policy change” link.

Jordan Lee: we can expose a “policy snapshot” endpoint that’s already computed by the control plane (safe subset). aggregator should fetch that.

Ethan Park: please make sure “safe subset” is explicit. policy configs can contain tenant annotations we don’t want sprayed in Slack.

Benji Okafor: for config diff, we’re planning allowlist-based rendering. everything else => “[redacted]”. secrets patterns are necessary but not sufficient.

Marcus Lin: exactly. regex-based “secret detection” will miss things and false-positive. allowlist primary, pattern-based secondary.

Allison Grant: on (2) caching: propose two layers?
- L1 in aggregator (in-memory) TTL 30-60s for interactive refresh spam
- L2 shared cache (redis) TTL 2-5m for same incident/thread to smooth bursts

Logan Wright: careful with caching telemetry. folks will treat it as real-time. if it’s 5m stale we need to label “as of” time and a refresh button.

Devon Price: UI-wise, we can show “Updated: 12:31:10Z” and “(cached)” in the card.

Rafael Mendes: + a note in degraded mode: “Live queries timing out; showing last known good context from 2m ago.”

Dylan Brooks: coalescing: yes. key should include {incident_id or thread_ts, service, region, tenant?}. if key is too broad we’ll leak tenant context.

Logan Wright: + on telemetry, avoid tenant in the query unless we need it. default to service/region aggregate for “saturation” card. tenant drill-down should be link out to Console where auth is stronger.

Miles Thornton: noted. current plan is:
- Saturation card = service/region/pool-level only
- Tenant-specific metrics only via deep link w/ RBAC

Aisha Bello: on (3) rate limits: we need budgets per thread and per user action.
- hard cap per thread: e.g. 6 refreshes / 5 min
- global cap in aggregator to protect upstreams
- backoff behavior: after cap hit, show message + links only

Allison Grant: also cap label cardinality. if telemetry query svc is asked for “by model, by tenant, by route, by status_code” we’re dead.

Logan Wright: we should define a strict set of queries the bot is allowed to run. like a fixed query catalog with parameters, not arbitrary promql.

Miles Thornton: yes, query catalog approach. aggregator calls telemetry-query svc with query_id + params (service/region/window). no raw query strings.

Ethan Park: +1. easier to permission and audit.

Kevin Osei: auditing: log (a) caller identity (bot service acct), (b) human user initiating action, (c) query_id, (d) params (redacted), (e) result size, (f) latency, (g) outcome (success/timeout/circuit-open).

Marcus Lin: and for config diff, log “fields returned” count maybe, plus whether any redactions occurred.

Benji Okafor: we can do “redaction_summary: N fields redacted” but keep it in audit logs not Slack.

Ravi Menon: on (4) partial outage behavior: define priorities.
- Must show *something* within ~10s (or slack will feel broken)
- If upstream slow: show cached + deep links
- If cached missing: show “manual commands” snippet (eg `rwctl deploys --service X --region Y`)

Connor O'Brien: for deploys, if deploy metadata svc is down, we can still pull from deploy event stream? maybe last-known state in redis.

Miles Thornton: we can cache deploy context longer (10-15m) since it doesn’t change second-to-second.

Allison Grant: but stamp it. “last deploy event seen at …”

Dylan Brooks: circuit breaker suggestion:
- per-upstream breaker in aggregator
- open when p95 latency > threshold or error rate > threshold
- when open: stop calling upstream for N seconds, serve cache + links

Logan Wright: yes. and telemetry-query svc should also have its own breaker to protect the TSDB.

Rafael Mendes: also, bot should never block incident channel during sev0. if it times out, it should say so plainly (not “unknown error”).

Devon Price: copy for error states:
- “Telemetry query timed out (10s). Showing cached context from 2m ago.”
- “Config diff unavailable: missing scope `config.diff.read`. Escalate via #help (INT ticket) or break-glass if Sev0/Sev1.”

Ethan Park: careful wording on break-glass. should route to the oncall/security process, not encourage random folks.

Naomi Feldman: agree. phrasing should be “Request elevated access via documented Sev0/Sev1 procedure (link).”

Kevin Osei: and the break-glass action itself must generate a high-signal audit event (and ideally page security-oncall if used outside sev0/1).

Miles Thornton: open question: should aggregator run in same cluster as bot or separate?

Logan Wright: separate. if bot gets wedged / restarts, we don’t want it taking cache + rate limiter with it.

Dylan Brooks: + separate deploy lets us roll diag changes without touching slack app bits. but keep it small.

Allison Grant: also consider: aggregator should have low dependency footprint. avoid calling 6 internal services. keep it to 3 + cache.

Priya Natarajan: platform POV: fine to add aggregator, but we need ownership clear. SRE owns? platform owns? shared?

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).

title: #eng-platform: 1739146122
preview:
Kaitlyn Nguyen: Need a decision on RBAC for route experiments before we lock the GA roles. Specifically: who can Start/Stop an experiment? And do we need a break-glass path for oncall?

Marissa Cole: +1. Beta customers asked “who in my org can stop an experiment if it’s blowing up prod.” We need a simple story in Console.

Sean Gallagher: For oncall: we *must* be able to stop quickly even if the customer team misconfigured RBAC / no one’s online. But also we can’t turn “stop” into “edit routing policy.”

Marcus Lin: Security perspective: “stop experiment” is a production-impacting action. Should be least-privileged but not blocked by needing full route admin.

Dylan Brooks: Current proposal in Console backend is `experiments:write` for create/update/start/stop. That’s too coarse.

Chloe Martin: Yeah right now it’s a single gate on mutation endpoints. We can split actions:
- create/update = `experiments:configure`
- start/stop = `experiments:operate`
- delete = `experiments:admin`

Jada Williams: Also note stop semantics: we have "stop (immediate)" and "stop (drain)" coming (state machine thread). If we expose drain later, that’s still an “operate” permission.

Rafael Mendes: If guardrail auto-stop triggers, that’s not a human. We should ensure audit logs show `actor=system` w/ reason `guardrail_violation`.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. The question is: do we tie operate permissions to *route ownership*? e.g. if you can operate a route, you can start/stop experiments on that route.

Marissa Cole: UX wise: I’d rather not force customers to manage a second permission set if they already have route-level roles.

Marcus Lin: But experiments can shift traffic between models/variants. That’s basically routing. If someone can stop/start experiments on a route they don’t own, they can still change prod behavior.

Sean Gallagher: During incident, we want the opposite: allow stop even if you don’t have route-level perms. But only for a tiny set of responders.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So maybe normal path: operate requires BOTH:
1) experiments:operate
2) route:operate for that route
…and then break-glass overrides #2.

Chloe Martin: Implementation-wise we can check:
- request has workspace role permitting experiments:operate
- AND the actor has access to route_id via existing policy ACL

Dylan Brooks: What about “stop only” as a separate permission? People keep asking for “let my support team stop but not start.”

Marcus Lin: +1. Split start/stop.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Something like:
```
experiments:start
experiments:stop
experiments:configure
experiments:delete
```
But we should map these to bundles in Console (Admin/Operator/Editor/Viewer).

Connor O'Brien: From release POV: fewer roles for GA. But we can keep internal fine-grain and expose 3-4 bundles.

Marissa Cole: Bundles I’d suggest:
- Viewer (read-only)
- Editor (create/update, not start/stop?)
- Operator (start/stop)
- Admin (delete + everything)

Rafael Mendes: Editor without operate is weird; you can stage config but can’t run it. Some customers will want that separation though.

Sean Gallagher: Staging config w/o operate actually matches “change management.” Platform team configures, SRE team operates.

Chloe Martin: We can make it optional in enterprise but default bundles for hosted. Still—backend permissions should support it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Ok, break-glass: what’s the actual flow? We can’t just say “SRE can do it” without process.

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: Redwood SRE “BreakGlass” role in our internal tenant only. Requires:
- MFA
- reason string (ticket/incident link)
- time-limited session (e.g., 1h)
Then can perform *stop* and *hard-disable* at feature-flag layer.

Marcus Lin: Make sure stop action is scoped. Break-glass should be able to stop an experiment by `experiment_id` without needing access to customer configs beyond what’s required. Also ensure redaction in debug endpoint.

Kevin Osei: For audit export: enterprise customers will want to see that Redwood used break-glass + reason. We should include `actor_type=redwood_operator`, `impersonation=false`, and a `support_case_id` field if provided.

Marissa Cole: Do we want customers to have their own “break-glass admin” too? Like an account-level owner who can always stop, regardless of route ownership.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s basically “Org Admin” for their workspace. We already have that in RBAC model. Question is: should Org Admin bypass route ACLs.

Marcus Lin: Careful. If Org Admin bypasses route ACLs, it can undermine route isolation across business units. Some enterprises explicitly want BU isolation even from central admins.

Dylan Brooks: But in practice, someone needs to recover the system. If we don’t allow bypass, they’ll file a support ticket anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Another angle: allow Org Admin to stop but not to start/configure across all routes. “Emergency stop” is safer than “emergency start.”

Sean Gallagher: +1, the safest universal action is "stop" (reduces blast radius). Start/configure should remain scoped.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: So policy:
- `experiments:stop` can be granted at workspace scope (Org Admin / Emergency Operator)
- `experiments:start` requires route-level operate
- create/update requires route-level admin (or route editor)

Chloe Martin: Implementation detail: stop endpoint currently does `PATCH /experiments/{id}:stop`. We can enforce:
- if actor lacks route access, allow only if actor has workspace emergency_stop flag
- and only if experiment is in RUNNING/DEGRADED (not DRAFT) to prevent messing with configs

Marcus Lin: Also log the diff: `status: RUNNING -> STOPPING` and include the reason. Stop reason must be captured from UI/API.

Marissa Cole: UX: we can add a modal for stop requiring a reason (optional for normal operators, required for emergency stop?).

Sean Gallagher: Required for emergency stop. Otherwise, people will click-stop without context during incidents.

Connor O'Brien: Can we get this locked today? Release train wants RBAC checklists.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Proposed decision (unless objections):
1) Split permissions: `experiments:configure`, `experiments:start`, `experiments:stop`, `experiments:delete`.
2) Default bundles exposed in Console: Viewer / Editor / Operator / Admin.
3) Start/configure scoped by route ACL (must have route access).
4) Stop allowed via route ACL OR via workspace-level “Emergency Stop” permission (no start/configure), with required reason + extra audit fields.
5) Redwood internal BreakGlass can exercise Emergency Stop; always audited with support/incident link.

Marcus Lin: Works for security if (4) is truly stop-only and audited. Add: break-glass sessions time-bound + MFA.

Sean Gallagher: Works for SRE. Also add a kill switch to disable hard-stop automation if we get false positives (separate thread but related).

Marissa Cole: Works for product. We’ll document “Emergency Stop” in enterprise RBAC docs.

Chloe Martin: I’ll update ENG-9029/9030 implementation notes and add tests for start vs stop perms. :thumbsup:

Rafael Mendes: I’ll update runbooks: who can stop, and where to find audit logs. Also add a note on stop reason.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Cool. I’ll write up a short RBAC section for the PRD + ping Kevin for audit export fields.

Kevin Osei: Please include: `emergency=true/false`, `reason`, `actor_role`, and `support_case_id` if present. I’ll sync with audit pipeline.

Connor O'Brien: Marking as decision for GA checklist. Thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1739128800
preview:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Also dot-notation keys can get weird depending on pipeline.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard + queries, flat is easier. Nested means our metrics extraction has to handle multiple shapes (and people will inevitably log arbitrary JSON).

Nadia Rahman: But we still need a way to carry producer-specific details (e.g., policy diff summary, installer operator metadata) without bloating the canonical field list.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we care about consistent actor/target + correlation first. Anything that breaks queries during an incident is a no.

Ethan Park: Control plane already has objects internally (`actor`, `target`, etc.). Flattening will be annoying but doable. My concern is “flattened forever” makes future evolution painful.

Allison Grant: Dropping some examples for discussion.

Allison Grant: Option A (nested-ish):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor": { "type": "user", "id": "usr_...", "email": "a@b.com" },
  "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role_..." },
  "request": { "request_id": "req_...", "trace_id": "trc_...", "source_ip": "1.2.3.4" },
  "extensions": { "policy_engine": { "diff": "..." } }
}
```

Allison Grant: Option B (flat):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_...",
  "actor_email_hash": "sha256:...",
  "target_type": "role",
  "target_id": "role_...",
  "request_id": "req_...",
  "trace_id": "trc_...",
  "source_ip": "1.2.3.4",
  "ext_policy_engine_diff": "..."
}
```

Kevin Osei: Strongly prefer Option B for SIEM export defaults. Customers can still parse JSON, but they shouldn’t have to.

Jordan Lee: Re: ext fields — can we use dotted names like `ext.policy_engine.diff`? Some SIEMs treat dot as nesting. Might be okay, but risky.

Kevin Osei: Yeah, Splunk: dotted fields become painful unless you normalize. Sentinel: depends on KQL ingestion rules. My vote: underscore prefix for namespacing: `ext_policy_engine_*`.

Hanae Suzuki: Also data classification/redaction is cleaner with flat + explicit fields. If we allow arbitrary nested “extensions”, people will accidentally drop raw payloads in there.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: forcing everything into `ext_*` strings feels like a junk drawer. Can we at least constrain ext keys via registry (namespaces + allowed fields)?

Logan Wright: We can do that. Registry can include canonical fields + allowed extension keys per namespace. Telemetry can track unknown keys to catch drift.

Allison Grant: Another angle: query ergonomics. In incident response we want: `event_type=... actor_id=... target_id=... source_ip=...` without JSON-path.

Marcus Lin: +1. During an investigation, people will be in Splunk/Sentinel at 3am. Flat fields win.

Nadia Rahman: If we go flat, can we keep a stable top-level set and allow extensions but still avoid huge cardinality? Some fields like `diff` can explode.

Allison Grant: We’ll enforce cardinality constraints in the standard (cap lengths, forbid free-form blobs unless explicitly approved). For diffs we can store a summarized diff + optional hash pointer.

Jordan Lee: So for policy changes: `ext_policy_engine_diff_summary` + `ext_policy_engine_diff_sha256`? Then full diff goes to internal debug logs only, not audit.

Hanae Suzuki: Works for PII too. If someone wants `actor_email`, we say no; only `actor_email_hash` if needed for correlation.

Ethan Park: Implementation detail: control plane services already build an `AuditEvent` struct. If we pick flat, we should provide a lib that takes nested internal structs and outputs the flattened schema.

Allison Grant: That’s already in flight (schema library + validator). We can add helper methods: `SetActor(...)`, `SetTarget(...)`, `SetRequestContext(...)`, and `AddExtension(namespace, key, value)` which emits `ext_<namespace>_<key>`.

Kevin Osei: +1 please also add exporter normalization: if some legacy producer still emits `actor.id`, exporter maps to `actor_id` etc.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard: we’ll key off `event_type` + `schema_version` + `service_name` (also needs to be top-level). If `service_name` is nested we’re back to parsing.

Allison Grant: Good call: `service_name`, `service_version`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private) should be top-level.

Marcus Lin: Any objections to: “Canonical top-level stable fields + flat namespaced extensions, no nested objects in v1”? (Exceptions only if a field is *always* a scalar and widely supported.)

Ethan Park: I’m good with that as long as we: (1) reserve prefixes, (2) provide registry + lints, (3) document how to evolve without breaking.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also let’s explicitly state “extensions are optional; consumers must not rely on them for core audit semantics.”

Kevin Osei: Works. From customer side we can say: stable top-level schema for SIEM; optional extensions for richer context.

Allison Grant: Decision: v1 will use **flattened top-level canonical fields** and allow **namespaced extensions as additional top-level fields** using `ext_<namespace>_<field>` (underscores, not dots). No nested JSON objects for actor/request/target; represent those as scalar fields (`actor_type`, `actor_id`, etc.). We’ll capture this in the ADR + logging standard and update the schema draft accordingly.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll update the coverage extractor to whitelist `ext_*` but ignore them for the “required fields present” check.

Hanae Suzuki: :+1: I’ll add redaction guidance: extensions cannot include secrets/PII; require review for any new `ext_*` key.

Kevin Osei: I’ll note in exporter docs: if customer wants nested output, they can transform downstream, but Redwood export is flat by default.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll post a follow-up with the reserved namespaces list (console, control_plane, policy_engine, private_installer, gateway, exporter) and the lint rule proposal.
text:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Also dot-notation keys can get weird depending on pipeline.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard + queries, flat is easier. Nested means our metrics extraction has to handle multiple shapes (and people will inevitably log arbitrary JSON).

Nadia Rahman: But we still need a way to carry producer-specific details (e.g., policy diff summary, installer operator metadata) without bloating the canonical field list.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we care about consistent actor/target + correlation first. Anything that breaks queries during an incident is a no.

Ethan Park: Control plane already has objects internally (`actor`, `target`, etc.). Flattening will be annoying but doable. My concern is “flattened forever” makes future evolution painful.

Allison Grant: Dropping some examples for discussion.

Allison Grant: Option A (nested-ish):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor": { "type": "user", "id": "usr_...", "email": "a@b.com" },
  "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role_..." },
  "request": { "request_id": "req_...", "trace_id": "trc_...", "source_ip": "1.2.3.4" },
  "extensions": { "policy_engine": { "diff": "..." } }
}
```

Allison Grant: Option B (flat):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_...",
  "actor_email_hash": "sha256:...",
  "target_type": "role",
  "target_id": "role_...",
  "request_id": "req_...",
  "trace_id": "trc_...",
  "source_ip": "1.2.3.4",
  "ext_policy_engine_diff": "..."
}
```

Kevin Osei: Strongly prefer Option B for SIEM export defaults. Customers can still parse JSON, but they shouldn’t have to.

Jordan Lee: Re: ext fields — can we use dotted names like `ext.policy_engine.diff`? Some SIEMs treat dot as nesting. Might be okay, but risky.

Kevin Osei: Yeah, Splunk: dotted fields become painful unless you normalize. Sentinel: depends on KQL ingestion rules. My vote: underscore prefix for namespacing: `ext_policy_engine_*`.

Hanae Suzuki: Also data classification/redaction is cleaner with flat + explicit fields. If we allow arbitrary nested “extensions”, people will accidentally drop raw payloads in there.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: forcing everything into `ext_*` strings feels like a junk drawer. Can we at least constrain ext keys via registry (namespaces + allowed fields)?

Logan Wright: We can do that. Registry can include canonical fields + allowed extension keys per namespace. Telemetry can track unknown keys to catch drift.

Allison Grant: Another angle: query ergonomics. In incident response we want: `event_type=... actor_id=... target_id=... source_ip=...` without JSON-path.

Marcus Lin: +1. During an investigation, people will be in Splunk/Sentinel at 3am. Flat fields win.

Nadia Rahman: If we go flat, can we keep a stable top-level set and allow extensions but still avoid huge cardinality? Some fields like `diff` can explode.

Allison Grant: We’ll enforce cardinality constraints in the standard (cap lengths, forbid free-form blobs unless explicitly approved). For diffs we can store a summarized diff + optional hash pointer.

Jordan Lee: So for policy changes: `ext_policy_engine_diff_summary` + `ext_policy_engine_diff_sha256`? Then full diff goes to internal debug logs only, not audit.

Hanae Suzuki: Works for PII too. If someone wants `actor_email`, we say no; only `actor_email_hash` if needed for correlation.

Ethan Park: Implementation detail: control plane services already build an `AuditEvent` struct. If we pick flat, we should provide a lib that takes nested internal structs and outputs the flattened schema.

Allison Grant: That’s already in flight (schema library + validator). We can add helper methods: `SetActor(...)`, `SetTarget(...)`, `SetRequestContext(...)`, and `AddExtension(namespace, key, value)` which emits `ext_<namespace>_<key>`.

Kevin Osei: +1 please also add exporter normalization: if some legacy producer still emits `actor.id`, exporter maps to `actor_id` etc.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard: we’ll key off `event_type` + `schema_version` + `service_name` (also needs to be top-level). If `service_name` is nested we’re back to parsing.

Allison Grant: Good call: `service_name`, `service_version`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private) should be top-level.

Marcus Lin: Any objections to: “Canonical top-level stable fields + flat namespaced extensions, no nested objects in v1”? (Exceptions only if a field is *always* a scalar and widely supported.)

Ethan Park: I’m good with that as long as we: (1) reserve prefixes, (2) provide registry + lints, (3) document how to evolve without breaking.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also let’s explicitly state “extensions are optional; consumers must not rely on them for core audit semantics.”

Kevin Osei: Works. From customer side we can say: stable top-level schema for SIEM; optional extensions for richer context.

Allison Grant: Decision: v1 will use **flattened top-level canonical fields** and allow **namespaced extensions as additional top-level fields** using `ext_<namespace>_<field>` (underscores, not dots). No nested JSON objects for actor/request/target; represent those as scalar fields (`actor_type`, `actor_id`, etc.). We’ll capture this in the ADR + logging standard and update the schema draft accordingly.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll update the coverage extractor to whitelist `ext_*` but ignore them for the “required fields present” check.

Hanae Suzuki: :+1: I’ll add redaction guidance: extensions cannot include secrets/PII; require review for any new `ext_*` key.

Kevin Osei: I’ll note in exporter docs: if customer wants nested output, they can transform downstream, but Redwood export is flat by default.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll post a follow-up with the reserved namespaces list (console, control_plane, policy_engine, private_installer, gateway, exporter) and the lint rule proposal.

title: #eng: 1739035050
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Heads up: scheduling a Private DR game day rehearsal next week. Need to lock scenario + roles today/tomorrow so we can prep runbooks + staging env. Two candidates: (A) region loss / restore into clean cluster, or (B) cluster corruption (control plane state / k8s etcd issues). Thoughts? Also need IC/comms/scribe + operators.

Talia Benson: :eyes: What day/time are you aiming for? We should pick something that doesn’t overlap w/ release cut.

Rafael Mendes: Tentative Wed 10:00–12:00 PT. Will confirm once we choose scenario.

Sean Gallagher: For usefulness, I’d vote B first. We’ve had more “restore drill failed because cluster is weird” than actual region loss. But we can simulate region loss by treating it as “new cluster” anyway.

Miles Thornton: +1 that “region loss” is mostly an infra story (DNS/TLS, new cluster bootstrap). “Cluster corruption” forces us to prove the operator workflow: identify blast radius, decide etcd restore vs app-level restore, verify tenancy/routing policies.

Ethan Park: If we do B, can we constrain it to control-plane datastore corruption (e.g., config DB / state) rather than full etcd? Full etcd restore gets hairy and may turn into infra-only exercise.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. If the objective is validating our backup artifact + installer `restore apply`, we should avoid etcd snapshot restore unless we *explicitly* want to test infra runbook.

Neha Kapoor: What success metric are we measuring? If it’s RTO for “Private control plane back online + can serve requests”, then we should simulate loss of control-plane state and force a deterministic restore. Otherwise we’ll spend 2h debating k8s.

Rafael Mendes: Goal is: prove Day-2 toolkit + runbooks for enterprise. So: consistent backup, restore into fresh cluster, validate.

Vivek Kulkarni: From customer angle, “region loss” wording resonates. But if we can implement it as “we lost the cluster/control plane, here’s how we restore elsewhere” that’s fine. In VPC docs we’re positioning a same-region clean cluster restore as baseline.

Colin O'Donnell: If we pick “region loss” in the narrative, we should include DNS/TLS steps in the checklist. Even in staging, we can fake it w/ internal DNS record + cert rotation note.

Kira Thompson: I can draft the comms + run-of-show, but I need crisp scope: are we expecting people to touch IAM/KMS setup during the drill? Or assume backup bucket + KMS are already provisioned.

Hanae Suzuki: Please assume KMS is provisioned. Otherwise the drill becomes “debug IAM” for 90 min. We can still include a check that restore fails safely if decrypt can’t happen.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also we should explicitly state we are NOT restoring secret material, just secret refs, so we don’t end up passing around decrypted payloads in Slack.

Connor O'Brien: For on-prem/air-gapped, we still need to validate signed bundle verification at least once, but that might be a separate game day. For this one (VPC), we can keep it simple.

Fatima Noor: If we’re skipping air-gapped this time, can we at least add one “offline artifact import” step as optional? Like: download artifact to local runner, re-upload, then restore. We’ve seen customer workflows where bucket access is segmented.

Sean Gallagher: That’s a good “inject friction” step without turning it into full air-gap.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: can we make sure the drill outputs a single “validation report” artifact (json/text) + emits metrics (duration, success/fail, error class)? I want something we can pin to the dashboard later.

Sergio Costa: I can be on point for dashboards/metrics. If we know the scenario, I’ll pre-create a temp dashboard panel set for staging + alert rules disabled.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, proposed: Scenario narrative = “region loss” (primary cluster unavailable). Implementation = restore into clean cluster in same cloud account + region (to avoid cross-region IAM issues), using latest backup artifact. Injected failure = first restore attempt fails due to missing CRD/ordering (to test runbook steps).

Ethan Park: Missing CRD injection is realistic (we’ve seen it). But can we ensure it doesn’t require code changes mid-drill? We can just deploy the target cluster with an intentionally incomplete Helm install.

Sean Gallagher: We can do that. Staging target cluster: install base chart but omit `redwood-private-crds` (or whatever the package is called) and see if the restore tool catches it in preflight.

Marcus Lin: If preflight doesn’t catch it, that’s also useful info. But please don’t make it too “gotcha” unless we tell operators what failure we’re simulating.

Neha Kapoor: Agree. Failure injection should be discoverable via runbook, not tribal knowledge.

Talia Benson: Roles:
- IC: Rafael
- Ops lead (backup/restore commands): Sean?
- Control plane eng support: Ethan
- Security/KMS consult: Hanae
- Comms/scribe: Kira (or I can do scribe)
- Observability: Sergio
- Timekeeper: me
Does that work?

Rafael Mendes: Works for me. Sean, can you do ops lead?

Sean Gallagher: Yep. I’ll run the commands + narrate.

Ethan Park: I’ll be on standby to answer “what does this restore step do” and help interpret failures.

Hanae Suzuki: I’ll cover KMS/decrypt + key rotation questions if they come up.

Kira Thompson: I can do comms + scribe. Please confirm the audience: just eng/sre, or include Solutions?

Vivek Kulkarni: Would be great to include at least one Solutions person as observer (Markus/Colin). But keep it mostly internal so we can move fast.

Colin O'Donnell: I’ll join as observer and sanity-check steps against customer expectations.

Elliot Price: If there’s a doc gap surfaced, tag me and I’ll translate into a docs task. I’ll observe.

Allison Grant: Can we define explicit “done” criteria now?
1) Backup exists and passes integrity verify
2) Restore completes in <X minutes
3) Post-restore validation: API health, at least one test tenant config present, routing policy present, can generate a token (?)
4) Dashboard shows restore drill success + duration

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add “abort criteria” so we don’t burn 3h: e.g., if restore blocked by infra provisioning, we pause and file ticket.

Rafael Mendes: Draft success criteria:
- RPO: latest backup <= 24h old (for drill)
- RTO target: control plane healthy + smoke tests green within 60 min from “declare disaster”
- Validation: installer validation report is PASS; API health endpoints green; sample tenant + routing policy restored; can run a simple inference request from staging runner.

Miles Thornton: Add: “document decision point: when to use etcd restore vs app-level restore”. Even if we don’t execute etcd restore, we should practice the call.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. We’ll add a 5-min decision checkpoint early: “is this k8s corruption or app state loss?” For our scenario we choose app-level restore.

Talia Benson: Logistics: which env are we using? We should freeze changes during the window.

Rafael Mendes: Staging: `private-drill-usw2` (new). We’ll do change freeze during 9:30–12:30 PT.

Connor O'Brien: Please make sure the installer version + docs version match. Last time we had “restore flag renamed” drift. I can provide the pinned artifact (installer + checksum) for the drill.

Kira Thompson: Perfect. If you send me the pinned version + links, I’ll put them into the run-of-show doc.

Sergio Costa: If you can paste the environment name + namespace(s), I’ll wire metric selectors. Also: do we want to emit a synthetic “restore_drill” label so we don’t pollute normal backup SLO charts?

Allison Grant: Yes, label it. `drill=true` or `event=dr_game_day`.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
- Sean: propose exact step list + preflight checks by EOD
- Connor: pinned installer artifact + checksums by EOD
- Sergio/Allison: define metrics + minimal dashboard for the drill
- Kira: comms + run-of-show doc (roles, timeline, success/abort)
- Ethan/Hanae/Marcus: review failure injection + ensure we’re not handling secrets unsafely

Rafael Mendes: I’ll send calendar once we confirm scenario. :thumbsup:

(eng-bot): Reminder set by Talia Benson for “DR game day scenario locked + roles assigned” in 2 hours.

Marcus Lin: One more note: please avoid posting any real KMS key ARNs in thread; use redacted examples. Also make sure restore logs don’t dump decrypted headers.

Sean Gallagher: Ack. We’ll run everything from the staging runner and only paste sanitized snippets.

Talia Benson: Quick check: are we aligned on scenario name to share externally later? “Control plane restore to clean cluster (region loss simulation)”.

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep, that phrasing works.

Rafael Mendes: Locked. Scenario = “region loss simulation → restore into clean cluster”, with injected “missing CRD / ordering” failure. Roles as listed above.

title: #eng-platform: 1738983301
preview:
Talia Benson: Go/no-go for Unit Econ Dashboard v1 today. Can we get quick status checks on: (1) pipeline SLOs + alerts, (2) reconciliation report vs billing, (3) access controls (feature flag + RBAC) ✅/⚠️

Dylan Brooks: +1. We should be crisp: if any of these are red we slip the broader rollout. Internal dogfood can continue, but we shouldn’t tell execs it’s "ready".

Logan Wright: Telemetry/aggregation side: hourly job is stable since yesterday’s backfill throttling change. Freshness looks good in the last 24h.

Aisha Bello: SLO dashboards: we’re tracking hourly completeness + freshness. Current status:
- Freshness SLO (p95 delay): green
- Completeness (hours with non-zero output tokens): green
- Missing-dimension % (region/tier/model): region is green, tier is *yellow* for the last hour (looks like a small spike in "unknown")

Allison Grant: ^ looked at the tier "unknown" spike. It’s mostly a single ingest shard lagging the tier enrichment join. It recovered on its own ~10m later. Alert fired but resolved.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV, alerting is in place and paging policy is set. We tuned thresholds to avoid noise, but this one is exactly what we want to catch. Runbook link is in the alert payload now.

Caleb Johnson: Data quality checks are deployed:
- token drop anomaly detection (per model/region)
- missing hours detection
- cardinality guardrails (quant_profile, route)
- reconciliation delta monitor (daily)
All passing in prod as of this morning.

Talia Benson: Can someone paste the actual SLO targets we’re holding ourselves to for launch?

Aisha Bello: Launch gates we agreed:
- Hourly pipeline freshness: 99% of hours available within 90 minutes (rolling 7d)
- Completeness: <0.5% of hourly buckets missing output_token totals (rolling 7d)
- Missing dimension coverage: <1% "unknown" for model + region + tier (rolling 24h), with "confidence" indicator shown in UI if above
We’re currently inside all, except the brief tier blip (maxed at ~1.3% for ~1 hour, then back).

Dylan Brooks: The tier blip isn’t ideal but it’s not a fundamental gap. If it’s transient and we show confidence, I’m ok.

Laura Bennett: Finance side: before we say "go", I need confirmation reconciliation report is within tolerance for QTD. The last time I checked we were off by more than I expected.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. Specifically: totals by day for hosted vs dedicated should tie out to billing aggregates within the agreed variance, and we need rate card version pinned.

Mei Lin: Reconciliation report status:
- Report is generated daily and also for an ad hoc date range
- Using rate card version 2025Q4 (explicit)
- Current variance (last 7 days):
  - Hosted: +0.8% tokens vs billing meter (known streaming late-arrival window)
  - Dedicated: -0.4% vs billing
  - Private: not in scope for billing tie-out (contracted) but totals match metering
Overall blended $/1M output tokens difference vs FP&A workbook is within ~1.1%.

Rishi Malhotra: The +0.8% is acceptable if it’s explained and stable. Is that the "streaming late arrival" issue or are we double counting?

Nadia Rahman: Not double counting. It’s timing: streaming emits output token increments throughout request; some increments arrive after the hour boundary. Aggregation has a 90m lateness allowance, but billing is day-bucketed and includes late arrivals. If you compare on day level, it tightens.

Mei Lin: Confirmed: day-level variance for Hosted drops to +0.2%.

Laura Bennett: Ok. Please make sure the dashboard defaults align with the finance view (day boundaries, timezone). We cannot have execs comparing UTC hours to PST day numbers.

Felix Schneider: UI currently defaults to "Last 7 days" and groups by day for the main chart, timezone = "America/Los_Angeles" for internal users. You can switch to hourly in the drilldown.

Marissa Cole: Also, the UI has the banner: "Internal metric (v1). Use reconciliation view for finance reporting." Is that still what Finance wants?

Rishi Malhotra: Close. Can we tweak wording to: "Finance-aligned definition. See Reconciliation for tie-out + known limitations". I don’t want it to sound like it’s not finance-aligned.

Nora Ahmed: Can do. I’ll update the copy and ship behind the feature flag.

Talia Benson: Great. Access controls check: are we locked down to allowlist + audit logs?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC/feature flag status:
- Feature flag: `internal_unit_econ_dashboard`
- Allowed roles: Finance, Eng Platform, Console Eng, SRE (explicit groups)
- Default deny for everyone else
- Audit logs for page access + export actions are enabled
We tested with INT-1042 style scenario (permission denied) and it’s behaving.

Marcus Lin: Security review: ok with current restrictions. Reminder: don’t add any customer_id in top-N tables. Aggregations only. I reviewed the query API responses; they’re dimensioned by model/region/tier only.

Chloe Martin: Confirmed API enforces group_by allowlist and rejects customer_id and route_id groupings. Also rate limited + cached (separate TTLs for "live" vs "finance" views).

Grafana Bot: [unit-econ-pipeline] ALERT RESOLVED: Tier dimension unknown_rate > 1% (10m). Current: 0.12%. Runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/SRE/Unit+Econ+DQ+Runbook

Sean Gallagher: Nice, exactly what we want. Alert fired, included runbook, resolved without human intervention.

Dylan Brooks: So are we comfortable calling this a "go" for v1 rollout to the initial internal cohorts?

Laura Bennett: From Finance: go, with the condition that (1) reconciliation view is linked prominently, (2) rate card version is visible in the UI, (3) day-level default + PST timezone stays.

Rishi Malhotra: +1 go. And please keep the variance chart in reconciliation; it’s super useful.

Logan Wright: Telemetry/Analytics: go. We’ll keep watching the dimension coverage. If tier unknown spikes again we’ll patch the join lag.

Allison Grant: SRE/Obs: go. Oncall rotation is set; escalation path is documented. Please don’t expand access beyond allowlist until we’ve had 1 week of green.

Talia Benson: Decision: GO for v1 (internal-only, allowlisted). Conditions noted (UI copy tweak + rate card visibility + reconciliation link). Nora/Felix can you confirm ETA for the copy + rate card label change?

Nora Ahmed: Copy change today.

Felix Schneider: Rate card version label is already in the footer of the dashboard, but it’s subtle. I’ll move it next to the headline metric and re-ship.

Chloe Martin: I’ll coordinate the deploy window and ping here when it’s live under the flag.

Dylan Brooks: Thanks all. Talia, can you update the launch checklist and notify `#general` after deploy for dogfood v1?

Talia Benson: Will do. Next check-in Monday for post-launch health + any Finance signoff follow-ups.

Dylan Brooks: +1. We should be crisp: if any of these are red we slip the broader rollout. Internal dogfood can continue, but we shouldn’t tell execs it’s "ready".

Logan Wright: Telemetry/aggregation side: hourly job is stable since yesterday’s backfill throttling change. Freshness looks good in the last 24h.

Aisha Bello: SLO dashboards: we’re tracking hourly completeness + freshness. Current status:
- Freshness SLO (p95 delay): green
- Completeness (hours with non-zero output tokens): green
- Missing-dimension % (region/tier/model): region is green, tier is *yellow* for the last hour (looks like a small spike in "unknown")

Allison Grant: ^ looked at the tier "unknown" spike. It’s mostly a single ingest shard lagging the tier enrichment join. It recovered on its own ~10m later. Alert fired but resolved.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV, alerting is in place and paging policy is set. We tuned thresholds to avoid noise, but this one is exactly what we want to catch. Runbook link is in the alert payload now.

- token drop anomaly detection (per model/region)
- missing hours detection
- cardinality guardrails (quant_profile, route)
- reconciliation delta monitor (daily)

- Hourly pipeline freshness: 99% of hours available within 90 minutes (rolling 7d)
