title: #eng-platform: 1738972101
preview:
Marissa Cole: hey all — need to finalize GA defaults for route experiments this week. Specifically: required fields (what Console enforces vs warns), max variants, stickiness key precedence, and UI guardrails. Can we converge in this thread so Connor can lock release notes + defaults?

Dylan Brooks: +1. We keep getting questions from beta customers “what happens if I don’t set sticky key?” and “why did my split skew”. would love GA to be opinionated.

Jada Williams: re: backend: we can enforce a couple things at create/start time without risk. Others should be warnings to avoid breaking existing configs on upgrade.

Jordan Lee: current evaluator precedence (from PR-28433 + follow-ups):
1) explicit sticky key from request context (header / SDK-provided)
2) `customer_id` (tenant)
3) `end_user_id` (if provided)
4) `session_id` (if provided)
5) fallback: request_id (NOT stable; only to avoid “all in one bucket”)

Jordan Lee: the skew incident was when we fell back to something low-entropy / too-shared. request_id at least spreads, but breaks “same user stays in variant”.

Allison Grant: + reminder: we also have the “cardinality guardrail” on dimensions. We should keep assignment keys stable but not explode tags. Key choice affects our ability to debug but not tagging (we don’t tag the key, we tag experiment_id + variant_id).

Sean Gallagher: from SRE POV: GA default should *strongly* encourage explicit stickiness. if the platform quietly uses request_id, customers will think it’s sticky and we’ll get churny results + a bunch of “why is my p99 flapping” tickets.

Marissa Cole: agree. question is: do we BLOCK start unless a stable key exists? or allow start w/ warning?

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC-wise no impact, but we should think about “who can override warnings”. if we block, it’s hard stop for everyone.

Ethan Park: for control plane API, we can add validation tiers:
- hard validation errors on schema correctness / safety
- “start warnings” that require explicit acknowledgement in Console
API can return warnings list; Console can show “I understand” checkbox.

Tessa Morgan: UX suggestion: treat it like our rollout “dangerous config” banners. Warning + explicit confirm on Start (not on Create). Also show sticky key resolution order inline, so it’s not a black box.

Dylan Brooks: +1 on confirm-on-start. Beta feedback: people tweak config a lot; don’t want to fight the form on save.

Logan Wright: telemetry note: if we allow non-sticky fallback, we should label the experiment as “non-sticky assignment” so results view can show a disclaimer. Otherwise people will interpret deltas wrong. (We can infer from config whether `stickiness.required=true` and whether request header is present? The latter we only know at runtime; but we can show: “No explicit stickiness key configured”)

Marissa Cole: ok let’s enumerate defaults.

Marissa Cole: Required fields candidates:
- experiment name
- route(s)
- variants w/ target policy (model_id / deployment target)
- allocation sums to 100
- start/end time optional
- guardrails ???

Benji Okafor: schema currently requires: `experiment_id`, `display_name`, `routes[]`, `variants[]`, `allocations[]`, `status`. guardrails are optional. eligibility rules optional.

Jada Williams: for GA, I’d like to make these hard-required at Create:
- at least 2 variants
- allocation sum == 100, each allocation >= 1
- route_id normalized (template, not raw path)
- each variant must resolve to a valid target (model + compat check)

Jada Williams: at Start-time we should hard-block:
- overlapping *active* experiments on same route (unless we add priority). We’ve punted priority; safest is “one active experiment per route”.

Jordan Lee: +1. Multiple experiments on same route is undefined in evaluator right now. We can add later.

Dylan Brooks: do we allow “draft” overlapping? probably yes.

Ethan Park: yes: allow create/update drafts, block activation if overlap with active.

Marissa Cole: cool. max variants?

Dylan Brooks: beta asks: “can I do A/B/C/D?” some want 5+. but UI gets messy.

Allison Grant: from metrics + dashboards: >4 variants makes charts unreadable and increases query fanout. also higher risk of tiny slices => noisy guardrails.

Sean Gallagher: also guardrails. if someone does 10 variants @ 10% each, p99 regression detection becomes a pager farm.

Jada Williams: backend can support N but I’m fine with GA cap.

Marissa Cole: proposal: GA max variants = 4. (A/B/n up to 4)

Dylan Brooks: +1.

Tessa Morgan: +1. We can message as “up to 4 variants (more later)”. Add UI helper text.

Connor O'Brien: for release notes + docs, I can write: “Experiments support 2–4 variants” + “one active experiment per route”. just need final.

Marissa Cole: stickiness key defaults — we should be explicit.

Jordan Lee: for GA, I recommend:
- default `stickiness.mode = preferred` (not required)
- precedence order in docs/UI: `x-redwood-sticky-key` header > `end_user_id` > `session_id` > `request_id`
- DO NOT use `customer_id` by default for stickiness (it’ll pin all users in a tenant to the same variant if they don’t provide user/session)

Dylan Brooks: wait — today we include `customer_id` in precedence, right?

Jordan Lee: yes, today it’s in there. That’s part of the skew problem for B2B SaaS customers who don’t pass end_user_id. All traffic in a tenant collapses.

Sean Gallagher: +1000. customer_id as a fallback is basically “single bucket per customer”. explains the EU skew ticket.

Allison Grant: also makes experiments “look stable” while totally wrong.

Ethan Park: changing precedence is behavior change. can we do it safely for GA?

Jada Williams: if we only change default precedence when config doesn’t explicitly set stickiness_key, it’s safer. Existing experiments created during beta might rely on old fallback though.

Marissa Cole: what do we do for existing beta experiments when they GA-migrate?

Jada Williams: if config has `stickiness_key = customer_id` explicitly, keep it. If config is “unset” (default), switch to new default precedence. But we should surface a warning in Console on old experiments: “Assignment behavior updated in GA; set a stickiness key to lock behavior.”

Dylan Brooks: makes sense.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: any security implications of using end_user_id/session_id? we’re already carrying it in request context in some routes.

Marcus Lin: as long as we’re hashing it (we are) and not logging raw values. Also debug endpoint must redact inputs. Reminder: debug endpoint should return “hash_input_source=header/end_user_id/etc”, not the actual ID.

Jordan Lee: yep. debug endpoint currently returns source + hash prefix only. (see PR-28596 review comments)

Marissa Cole: ok, so updated GA precedence proposal:
1) `x-redwood-sticky-key` (explicit)
2) `end_user_id`
3) `session_id`
4) `request_id`
(no `customer_id` in default chain)

Jordan Lee: +1.

Sean Gallagher: +1.

Allison Grant: +1.

Ethan Park: +1, but we need a migration note + maybe a “compat” flag.

Connor O'Brien: I’ll add to GA checklist: “confirm stickiness precedence change + migration copy”.

Marissa Cole: guardrails defaults — what should be on by default?

Sean Gallagher: minimum: show recommended thresholds in UI, but don’t auto-stop by default. soft-stop banners + notify is fine.

Dylan Brooks: we have soft-stop by default today. hard-stop is optional behind a kill switch.

Jada Williams: yes. For GA: `guardrails.enabled=true` (meaning evaluation + banner), `auto_stop=false` default.

Logan Wright: for metrics, we can’t make guardrails required because some customers won’t have stable baselines on day 1. But we can require *at least one* guardrail when enabling auto-stop.

Marissa Cole: UI guardrails proposal:
- On Create: require 2–4 variants; allocation sum 100; no 0% variants; show warning if any variant <5%
- On Start: block if overlapping active experiment on any selected route
- On Start: if no explicit stickiness key configured, show “Results may be noisy / non-sticky assignment possible” warning + require confirm
- On Start: if any variant <5%, warning + suggest staged ramp (e.g., 95/5 then 90/10)
- On Results: if attribution drop rate > X%, show banner + link runbook

Allison Grant: + also warn if route_id is raw path (high cardinality). We decided to normalize to template; if we detect wildcards missing, show warning.

Jordan Lee: we can hard-block raw path at create time. we already have route normalization constraints in PR-28741 follow-up.

Ethan Park: +1: route identifiers should be route_id from registry, not arbitrary strings.

Tessa Morgan: for the “variant <5%” thing: keep as warning, not block. Some teams intentionally do 99/1 to smoke test.

Sean Gallagher: agree warning only.

Marcus Lin: audit logging: if we add “warnings acknowledged” at start time, we should record it in audit event metadata (“warnings”: [...], “acknowledged”: true). Helps in postmortems.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1, and RBAC: if an Org has “restricted ops”, we might later require a higher role to start with warnings. But not GA.

Marissa Cole: any disagreement on max variants = 4, one active per route, stickiness precedence change (remove customer_id) + start-time warning if not explicitly set?

Dylan Brooks: no disagreement.

Jada Williams: ok.

Jordan Lee: I’ll file a follow-up to update ADR-047 appendix / docs snippet with new default precedence + migration note.

Logan Wright: I’ll update the metrics taxonomy doc to include “non-sticky assignment disclaimer” requirement for results.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync with Rafa on alert banner copy + runbook link. also ensure dashboards default view groups by {route, experiment, variant} only.

Connor O'Brien: action items I’m tracking:
- Jordan/Jada: confirm evaluator precedence change + how it’s gated for existing beta configs
- Dylan/Tessa: UI warnings + acknowledgement on Start
- Marcus: audit event includes warnings acknowledged
- Marissa: confirm final GA defaults in PRD / release notes

Marissa Cole: ✅ decisions for GA defaults (posting here so we can reference):
- Variants: min 2, max 4 (hard enforced)
- Allocation: must sum to 100; each variant >=1% (hard enforced)
- Active overlap: only 1 active experiment per route (hard enforced at Start; drafts can overlap)
- Stickiness: default precedence = header `x-redwood-sticky-key` > `end_user_id` > `session_id` > `request_id` (customer_id removed from default chain). If no explicit stickiness configured, show Start-time warning + requires acknowledgement.
- Guardrails: soft-stop evaluation + banners ON by default; auto-stop OFF by default; auto-stop requires at least one guardrail threshold configured.
- UI guardrails: warn (not block) on tiny variant slices (<5%), and show staged ramp suggestions.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: + please make sure the warning copy is strong. “without a stable key, results may be invalid”.

Tessa Morgan: got it. I’ll draft copy + share in #design for quick review.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add the audit metadata note to ENG-9030 follow-up. also reminder: debug endpoint stays internal-only + RBAC.

Connor O'Brien: locking this in for the 2025-04 release train doc. thanks all.

Ethan Park: +1: route identifiers should be route_id from registry, not arbitrary strings.

Tessa Morgan: for the “variant <5%” thing: keep as warning, not block. Some teams intentionally do 99/1 to smoke test.

Sean Gallagher: agree warning only.

Marcus Lin: audit logging: if we add “warnings acknowledged” at start time, we should record it in audit event metadata (“warnings”: [...], “acknowledged”: true). Helps in postmortems.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1, and RBAC: if an Org has “restricted ops”, we might later require a higher role to start with warnings. But not GA.

Marissa Cole: any disagreement on max variants = 4, one active per route, stickiness precedence change (remove customer_id) + start-time warning if not explicitly set?

Dylan Brooks: no disagreement.

Jada Williams: ok.

Jordan Lee: I’ll file a follow-up to update ADR-047 appendix / docs snippet with new default precedence + migration note.

Logan Wright: I’ll update the metrics taxonomy doc to include “non-sticky assignment disclaimer” requirement for results.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync with Rafa on alert banner copy + runbook link. also ensure dashboards default view groups by {route, experiment, variant} only.

Connor O'Brien: action items I’m tracking:
- Jordan/Jada: confirm evaluator precedence change + how it’s gated for existing beta configs
- Dylan/Tessa: UI warnings + acknowledgement on Start
- Marcus: audit event includes warnings acknowledged
- Marissa: confirm final GA defaults in PRD / release notes

Marissa Cole: ✅ decisions for GA defaults (posting here so we can reference):
- Variants: min 2, max 4 (hard enforced)
- Allocation: must sum to 100; each variant >=1% (hard enforced)
- Active overlap: only 1 active experiment per route (hard enforced at Start; drafts can overlap)
- Stickiness: default precedence = header `x-redwood-sticky-key` > `end_user_id` > `session_id` > `request_id` (customer_id removed from default chain). If no explicit stickiness configured, show Start-time warning + requires acknowledgement.
- Guardrails: soft-stop evaluation + banners ON by default; auto-stop OFF by default; auto-stop requires at least one guardrail threshold configured.
- UI guardrails: warn (not block) on tiny variant slices (<5%), and show staged ramp suggestions.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: + please make sure the warning copy is strong. “without a stable key, results may be invalid”.

Tessa Morgan: got it. I’ll draft copy + share in #design for quick review.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add the audit metadata note to ENG-9030 follow-up. also reminder: debug endpoint stays internal-only + RBAC.

Connor O'Brien: locking this in for the 2025-04 release train doc. thanks all.

title: #docs: 1738965007
preview:
Elliot Price: Kicking off docs coordination for SIEM-ready audit export beta. We have a few artifacts in flight (integration guide, Security FAQ, retention/backfill language). Need ownership + review path so we don’t ship contradictory wording.

Elliot Price: Current drafts/targets:
- Internal CS/SE guide: Splunk HEC setup + troubleshooting
- Security FAQ (customer-facing-ish, via Sales enablement)
- Admin docs for Console: “Audit Export Settings” + “Status/Health” + “Test connection”
- Retention vs export responsibilities (GRC language)

Elliot Price: @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei can you confirm who in Security is primary reviewer for wording around (a) PII minimization/redaction and (b) delivery guarantees (at-least-once, dedup expectations)?

Marcus Lin: I can be primary on semantics + PII boundary. Kevin can do retention/backfill + compliance questionnaire language. Also want to make sure we don’t accidentally promise “exactly-once”.

Kevin Osei: +1. Happy to own the “what we retain vs what customer retains” section and any SOC2/ISO phrasing.

Miguel Santos: From CS side: we need a crisp “what to expect in beta” section + troubleshooting decision tree (Splunk 4xx/5xx, Datadog 403/429, S3 access denied). I can own the troubleshooting content if someone gives me the technical bullets.

Colin O'Donnell: I can provide technical bullets + screenshots for Splunk HEC (token, index, sourcetype) and common errors. Also note Splunk HEC will 429 hard; exporter backoff is non-trivial.

Naomi Feldman: Re: “beta limitations must be explicit”:
- Hosted + Dedicated only (no Private/on-prem in beta)
- Backfill window limited (we’re not doing unlimited historical export)
- Delivery is at-least-once; customer should dedup on `event_id`
- Some fields conditional (IP/UA only for certain event types)

Naomi Feldman: We should align on the exact backfill limit that we want to publicly state (and that matches product defaults).

Dylan Brooks: For Console admin docs: I can get you UI copy (empty states + “Test connection” flow). But please don’t document knobs we might hide behind FF (filter builder advanced predicates). We’re trying to keep v1 simple.

Elliot Price: Great. Proposing ownership:
- Console admin docs (how to configure + status): Elliot (with Dylan for UI copy)
- Splunk HEC internal guide (CS/SE): Colin (with Miguel for troubleshooting structure)
- Security FAQ: Elliot to draft, Security to review/sign off
- Retention vs export responsibilities: Kevin (with Elliot to format/publish)

Elliot Price: Review chain suggestion:
1) Eliot drafts -> 2) Security review (Marcus/Kevin) -> 3) CS review (Miguel) -> 4) PM final (Naomi) for scope/roadmap statements.

Marcus Lin: Works. For Security review, I specifically want:
- No raw request/response payloads in examples (avoid customer prompts, completion text)
- Explicit statement: audit export contains metadata about actions, not model I/O
- PII section: “source_ip/user_agent may appear where applicable” + mention redaction rules for actor identifiers (email hashing? depends on schema)

Elliot Price: On that last point: are we hashing emails/user identifiers in audit events? I thought we were storing actor as user_id + display name (if present) but not full email.

Kevin Osei: Current direction: actor should be stable internal ID + optional human-readable name. Email should not be in the canonical event by default. If any legacy events still include email, docs should say “may include username/display name depending on IdP configuration” but avoid promising email.

Marcus Lin: Yep. Also: do not include full IP if customer has IP anonymization enabled (some enterprises do). We can say “source_ip when available”.

Confluence Bot: Elliot Price shared a link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/SEC/PII+Redaction+and+Sensitive+Fields+Policy+for+Audit+Events

Elliot Price: Thanks. I’ll lift the approved language from that page.

Miguel Santos: Can we get a short “customer expectations” section we can paste into support responses?
Like:
- “If downstream is throttling, you’ll see lag; events will arrive later (not lost).”
- “If you rotate credentials, expect up to X min before exporter resumes.”
- “If you change filters, it doesn’t retroactively delete already delivered events.”

Colin O'Donnell: + add: Splunk HEC sometimes returns 200 but drops events later if index misconfigured. We should include “validate by searching for a known event_id” and recommend enabling ACK in Splunk if they insist (though we’re not supporting ACK in beta iirc).

Naomi Feldman: We are not supporting Splunk ACK in beta. Please don’t mention it as a supported mode. You can mention “Splunk-side settings may affect indexing; validate in Search”.

Dylan Brooks: Also: filter semantics doc should clarify evaluation time. If filters are applied in exporter, there may be a short window where old config is still in effect (config sync interval). Don’t promise instant.

Elliot Price: Good call. I’ll add: “config changes typically take effect within a few minutes” and avoid hard numbers.

Elliot Price: Open question: what do we say about delivery guarantees?
Draft language:
“Redwood provides at-least-once delivery for enabled export destinations. During retries or replay, duplicate events may be delivered. Each event includes a stable `event_id` so downstream systems can deduplicate.”

Marcus Lin: That’s the right shape. Add: “Ordering is not guaranteed across partitions/destinations.” And avoid “exactly-once” anywhere.

Kevin Osei: And add retention/backfill:
- “Redwood retains audit logs for X days (per plan). Export is near-real-time but not a replacement for retention requirements unless customer stores exported data.”
- “Backfill can export recent history up to N days (beta).”

Naomi Feldman: Retention period varies by plan + contract, so we need to be careful. Don’t put a single number unless it’s universally true. Prefer “per your plan/contract” and point to the retention policy doc.

Elliot Price: Got it. I’ll phrase as “Redwood retention is defined by your plan/contract; export is customer-controlled and customers are responsible for retention in their SIEM/storage.”

Miguel Santos: For beta cohort, customers are going to ask “how far back can we backfill?” We should pick a default statement like “up to 7 days” or “up to 24 hours” so CS isn’t improvising.

Naomi Feldman: For beta: we agreed to allow backfill up to 7 days max. Some tenants may get less depending on retention, but we can safely say “up to 7 days, subject to plan retention.”

Kevin Osei: Works. I’ll incorporate that in the retention doc and the FAQ.

Colin O'Donnell: For Splunk guide I’ll include a section:
- required fields for searching: `event_id`, `category`, `action`, `actor.id`, `resource.type`, `workspace_id`
- recommended Splunk sourcetype: `redwood:audit`
- index: customer choice

Dylan Brooks: Please also capture the Console status fields names so CS can map screenshots to actual UI labels. We’ve got: “Export lag”, “Last success”, “Last error”, “Paused (auto)” vs “Paused (manual)”.

Elliot Price: Can you DM me the exact UI strings once final? I’ll keep docs consistent.

Dylan Brooks: Yep, will do.

Elliot Price: Timeline: I want all docs in review by EOD Thu so we can publish internally before the next beta check-ins.
- Colin: Splunk guide draft by Thu noon?
- Kevin: retention language by Thu EOD?
- Marcus: can you review FAQ + admin docs Fri morning?

Colin O'Donnell: Thu noon doable.

Kevin Osei: Thu EOD ok.

Marcus Lin: Fri morning review ok. Tag me when ready.

Miguel Santos: I’ll start a troubleshooting appendix with placeholders and sync with Colin on Splunk + with someone on Datadog/S3 (I don’t have those failure modes memorized).

Elliot Price: I can add Datadog/S3 common errors based on recent tickets (403 key scope, S3 AccessDenied/KMS). I’ll also link to runbook for deeper triage.

Elliot Price: Action items:
- [Elliot] Draft Console admin docs + FAQ language (delivery guarantees, beta scope) + Datadog/S3 troubleshooting bullets
- [Colin] Splunk HEC internal integration guide (setup + validation + common errors)
- [Miguel] Troubleshooting appendix structure + CS-ready canned responses
- [Kevin] Retention vs export responsibilities doc + backfill wording (“up to 7 days, subject to retention”)
- [Marcus] Security review for PII + semantics + “don’t overpromise” pass

Elliot Price: I’ll post links here once drafts are in Confluence.

title: #eng-platform: 1738956625
preview:
Ethan Park: Trying to lock rollout sequencing for audit logging v1 so we don’t thrash across teams. Proposal: (1) schema lib + validator, (2) control plane producers (RBAC + API keys), (3) console backend + UI workflows, (4) private installer parity. Thoughts / gotchas?

Priya Natarajan: +1 on sequencing. The dependency graph is real: everyone needs the schema + event_type registry first or we’ll ship 4 shapes. Let’s make it explicit as a gate for downstream PRs.

Connor O'Brien: For release coordination: can we define “Phase gates” w/ checkboxes? I need something I can paste into the release train notes.

Ethan Park: Yep. Gate 0: merged schema library + JSON Schema artifact + validator. Gate 1: control plane emitting v1 events for RBAC + API key lifecycle behind feature flag (per-tenant). Gate 2: console emits/validates and UI flows audited. Gate 3: private installer emits install/upgrade/rollback/config-apply events.

Sahana Iyer: For Gate 0, we should also land CI lints early, even if “warn-only” initially. Otherwise folks will add new event types ad-hoc while we’re mid-migration.

Ethan Park: Agree. But please don’t block everyone day 1 — maybe warn-only for 1 week then enforce?

Marcus Lin: (dropping in) I’m aligned with warn-only → enforce. But we need an explicit date/commit for the switch, and an exception process (time-boxed) so we don’t end up with permanent carve-outs.

Diego Santos: From the parity angle: sequencing should minimize “Hosted emits event X but Private doesn’t” windows. If we do control plane first, that covers the biggest customer asks (RBAC + keys). But we need to communicate that Private parity is coming and how long.

Dylan Brooks: Console note: we’re currently attributing actor in 2 different ways (session user vs service token) depending on workflow. If schema lib lands first, we can normalize actor + impersonation fields before we stamp more events.

Chloe Martin: +1. Also, console backend is a major producer for “export config changes” + “retention/residency settings”. Those depend on the same actor attribution fixes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Auth/authz detail: control plane RBAC + API keys are the most sensitive, but they’re also the easiest to get wrong in terms of “actor vs subject”. Please make sure the schema library includes an explicit pattern for: actor (human/service), subject (user affected), and target resource.

Ethan Park: That’s in the latest draft. Actor = who initiated; subject = user/service account affected; target = resource being modified. For key events, never log key material, only key_id + last4 if we have it.

Leila Farouk: Testing sequencing: if Gate 0 doesn’t include fixtures/examples, Gate 1+ will emit “valid schema” but we won’t catch semantics regressions. Can we include example payloads + golden tests in the schema lib PR?

Sahana Iyer: +1. Golden tests also help CI enforce required fields without devs guessing.

Priya Natarajan: Risk mitigation list we should capture:
- Feature flag per tenant (and per deployment mode) so we can roll back producers without rolling back core services
- Exporter normalization path for legacy events so customers don’t get broken dashboards during transition
- Cardinality guardrails (allow-lists) before console starts emitting free-form strings
- Backpressure behavior defined (don’t block admin workflows if audit sink is down)

Ethan Park: On backpressure: we’re leaning at-least-once delivery in pipeline, but producer behavior is best-effort (don’t fail the request if audit publish fails). We’ll emit metrics + alerts for publish failures.

Marcus Lin: Make sure that’s consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. We’ve had past “private blocks install if telemetry down” surprises.

Diego Santos: For Private installer, we also need an offline-friendly buffer. Some customer installs have restricted egress. If export destination is unreachable, we should spool locally w/ bounded disk + explicit operator warnings.

Connor O'Brien: Can we put that in Gate 3 acceptance criteria? “Installer continues; audit events queued; admin sees warning; events flushed when export configured.”

Ethan Park: Yes.

Dylan Brooks: Console risk: we have multiple UI entry points for the same action (e.g., role edit from user page vs RBAC page). If we instrument UI + BE, we may double-emit unless we decide “only backend emits” for state changes.

Chloe Martin: Strong preference: backend emits for anything state-changing. UI emits only “view/access” events if we decide those are in scope later. For this project, sensitive actions are mostly mutations.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. If we double-emit now, customers will scream “duplicates” and we’ll be back in dedupe land.

Sahana Iyer: That ties to event_id/idempotency. If backend is the source of truth, generate event_id server-side; if exporter retries, it should preserve event_id so SIEM dedupe is possible.

Ethan Park: Yup. We’ll standardize event_id generation in the library.

Priya Natarajan: Proposed concrete sequencing / timeline (rough):
Week 1: schema lib + registry + examples + warn-only lints
Week 2-3: control plane (RBAC + API keys) + integration tests
Week 3-4: console backend producers + actor attribution cleanup
Week 4-6: private installer parity + buffering guidance
Parallel: exporter normalization + schema_version tagging

Connor O'Brien: This is perfect. I’ll convert to release gates with owners.

Ethan Park: Owners suggestion:
Gate 0: Ethan + Sahana
Gate 1: Ethan + Kaitlyn (actor semantics)
Gate 2: Dylan + Chloe
Gate 3: Diego + Vivek’s team (I’ll loop them)

Leila Farouk: Please add “Gate N: E2E tests in CI” as a cross-cutting item. We can stage them: hosted fixture first, private fixture second.

Marcus Lin: Also add “rollback plan” per gate. If Gate 2 causes unexpected volume/cardinality, we need a kill switch.

Dylan Brooks: We can add a config toggle in console backend to drop audit publish (still log metrics) for emergency.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Capture decisions + mitigations in the rollout doc and link here.

Ethan Park: Will do. I’ll post the draft plan in Confluence + ping for :eyes: today.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: (reaction)

Sahana Iyer: :white_check_mark: (reaction)

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. Let’s keep scope discipline — schema first, then producers. No bespoke fields without registry approval.

Ethan Park: +1. Closing the loop after I post the plan link.

(DeployBot): Reminder: CI “audit-event-lint (warn-only)” pipeline is available on branch `audit/schema-lib-rollout` (docs: go/redwood/audit-lint).

Sahana Iyer: That ties to event_id/idempotency. If backend is the source of truth, generate event_id server-side; if exporter retries, it should preserve event_id so SIEM dedupe is possible.

Ethan Park: Yup. We’ll standardize event_id generation in the library.

Priya Natarajan: Proposed concrete sequencing / timeline (rough):
Week 1: schema lib + registry + examples + warn-only lints
Week 2-3: control plane (RBAC + API keys) + integration tests
Week 3-4: console backend producers + actor attribution cleanup
Week 4-6: private installer parity + buffering guidance
Parallel: exporter normalization + schema_version tagging

Connor O'Brien: This is perfect. I’ll convert to release gates with owners.

Ethan Park: Owners suggestion:
Gate 0: Ethan + Sahana
Gate 1: Ethan + Kaitlyn (actor semantics)
Gate 2: Dylan + Chloe
Gate 3: Diego + Vivek’s team (I’ll loop them)

Leila Farouk: Please add “Gate N: E2E tests in CI” as a cross-cutting item. We can stage them: hosted fixture first, private fixture second.

Marcus Lin: Also add “rollback plan” per gate. If Gate 2 causes unexpected volume/cardinality, we need a kill switch.

Dylan Brooks: We can add a config toggle in console backend to drop audit publish (still log metrics) for emergency.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Capture decisions + mitigations in the rollout doc and link here.

Ethan Park: Will do. I’ll post the draft plan in Confluence + ping for :eyes: today.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: (reaction)

Sahana Iyer: :white_check_mark: (reaction)

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. Let’s keep scope discipline — schema first, then producers. No bespoke fields without registry approval.

Ethan Park: +1. Closing the loop after I post the plan link.

(DeployBot): Reminder: CI “audit-event-lint (warn-only)” pipeline is available on branch `audit/schema-lib-rollout` (docs: go/redwood/audit-lint).

title: #eng-platform: 1738948822
preview:
Ethan Park: Dropping this here for review before we merge the backup status API. PR is up: redwood/pr-24318 ("Expose backup status endpoints for Private"). Main thing: endpoint to surface `last_success_ts` etc so Console + automation can show DR readiness.

GitHub Bot: @eng-platform PR #24318 ready for review. Checks: unit-tests ✅ lint ✅

Priya Natarajan: Skimmed. The shape looks fine, but I’m worried about authz + tenancy isolation. Right now it’s `GET /private/v1/backups/status?tenant_id=...` (from the PR description). We should not accept tenant_id as a free param.

Benji Okafor: Yep, in code it’s `tenant_id` pulled from the JWT claims if present, param is only used by internal calls. But I agree it’s confusing.

Marcus Lin: +1 to Priya. Even if “internal”, params have a way of sticking around. Strong preference:
- derive tenant/install identity from auth context (mTLS client cert / service token / admin session)
- don’t allow a caller to enumerate tenants by guessing IDs
- if we need an admin override, make it a separate admin-only route behind `private:admin` scope.

Hanae Suzuki: Also, what’s the expected caller here in Private?
- Console inside the customer VPC?
- our support tooling?
Because token scope / audience needs to be explicit. We shouldn’t accidentally allow data-plane workload identities to call this.

Ethan Park: Intended callers:
1) Private Console backend (same control plane namespace)
2) Installer/automation (cron) to post status
3) SRE tooling in Redwood-managed staging
Not intended for customer apps.

Sean Gallagher: For SRE: we’ll likely scrape/poll this (or have metrics). If endpoint is cheap and rate limited, ok. But if it hits DB every time, we’ll DDOS ourselves with dashboards.

Allison Grant: From observability POV, I’d rather we have metrics emitted by the backup job and only use the API for “human view” / support diagnostics. But I get why Console wants it.

Priya Natarajan: Proposal:
- Route: `GET /private/v1/backup/status` (singular “this install”) w/ no identifiers.
- Response includes: `last_success_ts`, `last_attempt_ts`, `last_error_code`, `format_version`, `artifact_location` (maybe redacted), plus per-component statuses.
- If we need multi-tenant in the hosted control plane later, create a different surface (`/admin/...`).

Benji Okafor: That’s doable. Right now we support multiple tenants in the same Private control plane only in the sense of multiple “orgs” — but install is still one cluster. So “this install” endpoint works.

Marcus Lin: Tenancy isolation detail: make sure response doesn’t leak other tenants’ configured backup locations or KMS key IDs. I saw `kms_key_arn` in the response struct in the PR.

Benji Okafor: Good catch. That was meant for debugging, but we can remove or hash.

Hanae Suzuki: Please remove KMS identifiers from the API response. If we need to validate KMS integration, do it via a separate "kms health" check, not in a backup status endpoint.

Rafael Mendes: Rate limiting: can we set something like 1 rps per token, burst 5, and cache for 30s? Console might poll every page refresh + background.

Ethan Park: There’s already gateway rate limiting, but we can add a local cache. Right now handler calls BackupStatusStore (postgres) directly.

Sean Gallagher: I’d be happy with:
- 30s cache keyed by install_id
- response includes `computed_at`
- rate limit at edge (global + per token)
Also: please pick error codes we can page on. “string message” will end up in alerts.

Allison Grant: If we keep it, can we include a stable `last_error_category` enum? e.g. `STORAGE_AUTHZ`, `KMS_UNAVAILABLE`, `INTEGRITY_CHECK_FAILED`, `UNKNOWN`.

Benji Okafor: Yup. In PR it’s `last_error` freeform. I can change to `{code, category}` (but still keep it flat-ish).

Priya Natarajan: Keep it simple: `last_error_code` (stable string), `last_error_hint` (optional, human), and no stack traces.

Marcus Lin: Authz scopes: I didn’t see explicit scope checks. Please ensure:
- only control-plane service tokens with `private:ops:read` (or whatever we call it) can call
- customer admin UI sessions can call if they have cluster-admin (or Redwood Private admin role)
- deny by default
And please return 404 for “not configured” rather than 403/200 w/ empty fields? Helps avoid scanning patterns.

Ethan Park: PR currently checks `HasRole("private-operator")` for session auth, and service tokens go through mTLS allowlist. I’ll tighten scope naming + comments.

Hanae Suzuki: Also consider token audience. We’ve had issues where any internal token worked across services.

Benji Okafor: Ack. I’ll add audience validation to the middleware (we already have it for other private routes).

Sean Gallagher: One more: can we avoid putting `artifact_location` at all? That could be S3 bucket names / NFS paths. Support might want it, but it’s also sensitive.

Vivek Kulkarni: For customers, we don’t want them thinking we “manage” their bucket. Better to just show “backend: s3/nfs/gcs” + “retention: X days” and a doc link.

Marcus Lin: +1. Remove raw locations. If needed, we can include `backend_type` and `backend_id` as opaque (e.g., "default"), but no bucket names.

Benji Okafor: Makes sense. I’ll drop `artifact_location` and `kms_key_arn`. Keep:
- backend_type
- last_success_ts
- last_attempt_ts
- bytes_written
- duration_ms
- format_version
- last_error_code
- computed_at

Allison Grant: Add `job_name`? Helps correlate with metrics/logs. But ok if not.

Priya Natarajan: Let’s not add too many fields. If we need correlation, we can add `operation_id` later.

Ethan Park: Action items from this thread:
1) Remove tenant_id param; make endpoint “this install” only
2) Remove KMS key + raw artifact location
3) Add explicit scope/audience checks; deny by default
4) Add cache + rate limit notes
5) Switch last_error to stable error code/category

GitHub Bot: @Benji Okafor pushed commits to PR #24318.

Benji Okafor: Updates pushed:
- Route now `GET /private/v1/backup/status`
- Removed `tenant_id` query param
- Removed `artifact_location` + `kms_key_arn`
- Added `last_error_code` + `last_error_category`
- Added `computed_at` + in-memory 30s cache
Still need review on scope naming.

Marcus Lin: Reviewed the diff. Scope check still reads `private-operator` role. Please map that to a named scope in authz middleware (even if it’s internally implemented as a role). Otherwise docs/SEs won’t know what to request.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Also: confirm that for air-gapped installs, auth doesn’t attempt to introspect tokens externally.

Benji Okafor: Good point — no introspection. It’s local JWT verification + audience claim check. I’ll rename to `private:ops:read` in the middleware and keep role mapping behind the scenes.

Sean Gallagher: Cache looks good. Add a note in the runbook that polling should be <= 1/min unless troubleshooting.

Ethan Park: Sounds good. Once Benji updates scope naming, I think we can LGTM.

Priya Natarajan: Please add a short comment in code why we intentionally don’t include bucket names / key IDs (security + tenancy). Prevents regression in a future “helpful debug” PR.

Benji Okafor: Will do, pushing one more commit.

GitHub Bot: PR #24318 merged into main by @Ethan Park. ✅

title: #eng-releases: 1738894410
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Heads up: need to lock the rollout steps for SIEM-ready audit export beta for next release train (targeting Thu). We have control plane + exporter + Console pieces all feature-flagged, but we should agree on sequencing + rollback. Can folks confirm flags + who flips what for beta tenants?

Naomi Feldman: +1. Beta cohort is 3 accounts (Acme, Helio, Quantiva) but we’re enabling one-by-one. Need a super clear “enable checklist” for CSM/Solutions.

Ethan Park: From control plane side we have:
- `ff.audit_export.config_api` (CRUD endpoints, validation)
- `ff.audit_export.rbac` (permission gates; otherwise endpoints 404)
- `ff.audit_export.tenant_allowlist` (tenant-based guard)
There’s also a hard kill switch `ff.audit_export.global_disable` that forces exporter to stop pulling configs.

Sean Gallagher: For SRE: we’re ok with a limited beta *if* we have:
1) dashboards live before enabling any customer
2) paging policy agreed (what pages vs ticket)
3) rollback = “pause exports” is one click / one flag
Also can we avoid flipping during train deploy? prefer daytime PT.

Rafael Mendes: +1. We need to align on what rollback looks like if Splunk/Datadog start 429’ing and lag explodes again. “Disable tenant allowlist entry” vs “global disable” have very different blast radius.

Logan Wright: Observability pack dashboards are in the same train. We’ll have:
- export lag (p50/p95) by destination type
- adapter error rate + 429s
- retries / DLQ count
- throughput (events/s)
If we enable customers before dashboard deploy, we’re blind.

Dylan Brooks: Console UI is behind `ff.audit_export.console_settings_ui`. If flag is off, page is hidden. If on but tenant not allowlisted, page shows “not enabled” empty state.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC detail: the new perms are behind `ff.audit_export.rbac`, but the policy bundle defaults will ship in the train too. If we flip the UI without policy bundle, admins might see the page but get permission errors. Please sequence policy bundle first.

Marcus Lin: Security ask: DO NOT tell anyone “exactly-once”. It’s at-least-once w/ dedup downstream via `event_id`. Rollback plan should include “export may be paused; audit logs retained in Redwood per retention policy; backfill window is limited.”

Connor O'Brien: Ok, proposal for train sequencing (deploy artifacts) vs customer enablement (flag flips):

A) Release train deploy order (automated):
1. policy bundle (RBAC perms)
2. control plane (config API + status endpoint)
3. exporter service
4. Console UI
5. dashboards/alerts

B) Post-deploy enablement steps (manual, per tenant):
1. confirm `ff.audit_export.global_disable = false`
2. enable `ff.audit_export.rbac` + `ff.audit_export.config_api`
3. add tenant to `ff.audit_export.tenant_allowlist`
4. enable `ff.audit_export.console_settings_ui`
5. have customer set destination + run “test connection”
6. verify exporter status endpoint shows green + events flowing

Rollback:
- fast per-tenant: remove tenant from allowlist (stops new pulls, exporter pauses for that tenant)
- fast global: set `ff.audit_export.global_disable = true` (exporter pauses all tenants)
- if train itself bad: revert deploy per normal rollback runbook

Can everyone sanity check?

Sean Gallagher: This looks fine. One nuance: for rollback, allowlist removal should also stop retries quickly. Does exporter poll allowlist frequently? If not, we need “pause” to take effect within minutes.

Ethan Park: Exporter reads allowlist via config sync; TTL is 60s w/ jitter. So within ~1–2 mins it should stop pulling. If we need immediate stop, global_disable is instant because exporter checks that locally every loop.

Rafael Mendes: Suggest we define explicit triggers:
- if p95 lag > 15m for 10m => page SRE, consider tenant pause
- if adapter 5xx > 5% for 5m OR sustained 429 storm => pause tenant
- if exporter CPU/mem thrash impacts other services => global_disable
And who has permissions to flip global_disable? should be on-call only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. Limit global_disable to SRE + releases. Tenant allowlist can be Support/CS via the internal tool (audited). Please make sure flips are audited as well.

Naomi Feldman: For beta, I want “tenant pause” as the default rollback. Global disable only if platform health is at risk. I’ll add to beta criteria doc.

Logan Wright: Dashboard availability: we can have the panels live right after deploy. Alerts: we should start with ticketing alerts for lag for beta, not paging, unless it’s platform risk.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Paging only on “platform risk” conditions. Otherwise tickets + Slack notify in #eng-sre.

Dylan Brooks: Console “test connection” currently only validates auth + endpoint reachability; it doesn’t guarantee delivery. We should note that in enable checklist to avoid false confidence.

Marcus Lin: Also re: logging/redaction: ensure exporter logs do not print full destination URLs w/ query params or tokens. Rollback steps should include “rotate credentials” path if we suspect leak.

Connor O'Brien: Great. Concrete plan for Thu train:
- Wed EOD: confirm all PRs merged + feature flags default OFF
- Thu 10am PT: train deploy
- Thu 1pm PT: verify dashboards/health
- Thu 2pm PT: enable *single* tenant (Helio) + monitor 24h
- Fri: enable Acme if green
- Next week: enable Quantiva (EU residency questions outstanding)

Please reply with any blockers by end of day.

DeployBot: Train RT-2025.02.06 queued for Thu 10:00 PT. Change set includes: redwood@pr-28824, audit-log-exporter@pr-1119, observability-pack@pr-392, rbac-policy-bundle@pr-244

Ethan Park: Blocker: we need one more migration guard (we saw configs disabling unexpectedly when toggling allowlist). Fix is in flight but should land before Wed.

Rafael Mendes: If that fix misses, do NOT enable tenants. The risk is silent disable -> customer thinks exports are on but nothing delivered.

Connor O'Brien: Ack. We’ll gate customer enablement on that fix. Train can still ship with flags OFF.

Naomi Feldman: Works for me. I’ll update CSMs: “beta shipping in train, enablement pending final guardrail.”

Sean Gallagher: Can you add the rollback snippet to the on-call handoff doc? Just the exact flags and where to flip them.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, will post a short checklist here after I finalize with SRE.

Connor O'Brien: Rollback quick ref (beta):
1) Pause single tenant (preferred): remove tenant from allowlist in Admin Tool → Feature Flags → `audit_export.tenant_allowlist` (takes effect ~1–2 mins)
2) Emergency pause all exports: set `audit_export.global_disable = true` (effect <30s)
3) If destination creds compromised: rotate creds in Console + invalidate old secret ref; confirm exporter resumes; if not, keep tenant paused and open INT ticket
4) After any rollback: capture exporter status payload + dashboard screenshots; notify #support and Security if data exposure suspected

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: add link to runbook when you have it.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: + please ensure global_disable is restricted; no broad CS access.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll confirm permissions in policy bundle. global_disable should be `releases-admin` + `sre-oncall` only.

Marcus Lin: Security sign-off condition: enablement checklist must include “confirm PII redaction enabled” and “no debug logging” for exporter. If someone wants to temporarily increase log level, we need security review.

Connor O'Brien: Noted. I’ll incorporate. Thread can go quiet unless more blockers show up.

Logan Wright: Dashboard availability: we can have the panels live right after deploy. Alerts: we should start with ticketing alerts for lag for beta, not paging, unless it’s platform risk.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. Paging only on “platform risk” conditions. Otherwise tickets + Slack notify in #eng-sre.

Dylan Brooks: Console “test connection” currently only validates auth + endpoint reachability; it doesn’t guarantee delivery. We should note that in enable checklist to avoid false confidence.

Marcus Lin: Also re: logging/redaction: ensure exporter logs do not print full destination URLs w/ query params or tokens. Rollback steps should include “rotate credentials” path if we suspect leak.

Connor O'Brien: Great. Concrete plan for Thu train:
- Wed EOD: confirm all PRs merged + feature flags default OFF
- Thu 10am PT: train deploy
- Thu 1pm PT: verify dashboards/health
- Thu 2pm PT: enable *single* tenant (Helio) + monitor 24h
- Fri: enable Acme if green
- Next week: enable Quantiva (EU residency questions outstanding)

Please reply with any blockers by end of day.

DeployBot: Train RT-2025.02.06 queued for Thu 10:00 PT. Change set includes: redwood@pr-28824, audit-log-exporter@pr-1119, observability-pack@pr-392, rbac-policy-bundle@pr-244

Ethan Park: Blocker: we need one more migration guard (we saw configs disabling unexpectedly when toggling allowlist). Fix is in flight but should land before Wed.

Rafael Mendes: If that fix misses, do NOT enable tenants. The risk is silent disable -> customer thinks exports are on but nothing delivered.

Connor O'Brien: Ack. We’ll gate customer enablement on that fix. Train can still ship with flags OFF.

Naomi Feldman: Works for me. I’ll update CSMs: “beta shipping in train, enablement pending final guardrail.”

Sean Gallagher: Can you add the rollback snippet to the on-call handoff doc? Just the exact flags and where to flip them.

Connor O'Brien: Yep, will post a short checklist here after I finalize with SRE.

Connor O'Brien: Rollback quick ref (beta):
1) Pause single tenant (preferred): remove tenant from allowlist in Admin Tool → Feature Flags → `audit_export.tenant_allowlist` (takes effect ~1–2 mins)
2) Emergency pause all exports: set `audit_export.global_disable = true` (effect <30s)
3) If destination creds compromised: rotate creds in Console + invalidate old secret ref; confirm exporter resumes; if not, keep tenant paused and open INT ticket
4) After any rollback: capture exporter status payload + dashboard screenshots; notify #support and Security if data exposure suspected

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: add link to runbook when you have it.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: + please ensure global_disable is restricted; no broad CS access.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: I’ll confirm permissions in policy bundle. global_disable should be `releases-admin` + `sre-oncall` only.

Marcus Lin: Security sign-off condition: enablement checklist must include “confirm PII redaction enabled” and “no debug logging” for exporter. If someone wants to temporarily increase log level, we need security review.

Connor O'Brien: Noted. I’ll incorporate. Thread can go quiet unless more blockers show up.

title: #eng-security: 1738783321
preview:
Hanae Suzuki: final push before we publish the Confluence docs + ADRs for audit logging. can folks do a last review pass today/tomorrow? dropping checklist + who i think should sign off.

Hanae Suzuki: ✅ Final review checklist (please reply w/ :thumbsup: when done)
1) Confluence pages present + consistent
- audit-log-schema-v1.json page: required fields, examples, JSON schema link works
- sensitive-actions-event-taxonomy.json page: event_type naming consistent w/ registry
- audit-log-coverage-definition.json page: “100% coverage” definition + exclusions process
- audit-log-versioning-and-backcompat-policy.json page: v1 → v1.x rules, deprecation window
- audit-logging-redaction-and-data-classification.json page: no secrets/PII guidance, hashing rules
- audit-logging-control-mapping-soc2-iso.json page: control IDs + evidence examples
- audit-log-events-logging-standard.json page: eng standard + failure semantics
- ADRs: adr-audit-log-schema-and-event-taxonomy + adr-audit-event-delivery-guarantees

2) Cross-links + nav
- each page links to the others (esp schema ↔ taxonomy ↔ logging standard)
- ADR pages link back to Confluence standards
- runbooks referenced where applicable (pipeline incident, coverage regression)

3) Content sanity
- “actor/target/resource” terminology consistent everywhere
- schema_version language consistent (no “vNext” / “TBD” left behind)
- no customer-facing promises on delivery semantics beyond what we can back (at-least-once vs best-effort wording)

4) Examples validate
- example events conform to schema (ideally run through validator)
- examples do NOT contain: API keys, tokens, prompt text, email addresses (unless explicitly allowed + hashed)

5) Cardinality + field constraints called out
- any free-form strings have guidance (diff_summary is capped, user_agent capped, etc)
- enumerations for event_type + action + outcome documented

Proposed reviewers / sign-off:
- Schema + redaction: @Marcus Lin @Hanae Suzuki
- Observability standard + delivery wording: @Allison Grant
- Control mapping / evidence: @Olga Petrov
- SIEM/export guidance references: @Kevin Osei
- Coverage/dashboard language: @Logan Wright
- Editorial pass / doc quality: @Elliot Price
- Product wording for external shareables: @Naomi Feldman

Timeline target:
- Review complete by Thu 2pm PT
- Publish to Confluence Thu EOD
- Announce in #eng + #security-compliance Fri AM

Marcus Lin: +1. thanks Hanae. for schema/redaction: i’ll focus on “don’t log secrets” + actor/impersonation bits. pls flag anything that sounds like we’re guaranteeing 0-drop.

Allison Grant: i can take delivery wording + logging standard. quick q: are we explicitly saying “audit event emission is non-blocking” for admin workflows? that was the last debate.

Hanae Suzuki: yeah, currently says “non-blocking; buffered best-effort; critical paths should continue even if sink degraded” but also references at-least-once downstream when ingestion is healthy. would love your eyes on that section.

Olga Petrov: i’ll review the SOC2/ISO mapping page. can you confirm which screenshots/queries we’re comfortable committing to for evidence? last time auditors asked for exact query examples.

Hanae Suzuki: yep—page has “sample queries (non-binding)” and separates “required evidence” vs “suggested artifacts.” but please tweak if too hand-wavy.

Kevin Osei: i’ll check exporter bits + anywhere we mention Splunk/Sentinel. also want to ensure we instruct customers to de-dupe by event_id (and not request_id).

Logan Wright: on coverage language: i’ll verify the “expected vs observed event types” section lines up with what ENG-4828 is building. we should avoid implying realtime completeness checks if the dashboard is delayed.

Elliot Price: i can do an editorial pass + link hygiene. do we have a standard page label/tag set? (so folks can find these later)

Hanae Suzuki: good call. i tagged with: “security”, “compliance”, “audit-logging”, “observability-standards”. if there’s a canonical label list, i’ll align.

Sahana Iyer: i’m not on the official reviewer list but i can skim for CI/lint mentions. i saw one section referencing “build-time” lints—just want to ensure it doesn’t promise it’s already enforced everywhere.

Hanae Suzuki: please do 🙏. there’s a sentence like “CI will fail for unregistered event_type” which is true for new producers but not for legacy yet. we should tighten wording.

Naomi Feldman: i’ll review anything that could end up in external security FAQ / customer packets. specifically: retention statements, export targets, and “coverage” claims. please avoid “100% today” language unless scoped to “sensitive actions defined in taxonomy v1”.

Vivek Kulkarni: can i sanity-check Private parity statements? we’ve had customers interpret “same as hosted” as “all features day 0”. i’ll look for wording like that.

Hanae Suzuki: yes pls. Private sections are intentionally cautious but would love your stamp.

Marcus Lin: also: can we add a small callout on duplicates? retries => duplicates are expected; consumer should de-dupe on event_id. support keeps getting this question.

Kevin Osei: +1. i’ll add a short note in versioning/backcompat policy + exporter section: “at-least-once delivery; duplicates possible; use event_id for idempotency”.

Allison Grant: reviewed logging standard doc. changes requested:
- replace “guaranteed delivery” w/ “target delivery SLO”
- add explicit behavior during sink outage: queue up to N, then drop w/ metric + alert (don’t block request)
- add note: event_time vs observed_time semantics

Hanae Suzuki: ack. can you paste exact wording you want for the “sink outage” paragraph? i’ll incorporate.

Allison Grant: suggested text:
“Services MUST NOT block customer/admin requests on audit sink availability. Producers SHOULD buffer audit events locally up to a bounded limit; on overflow they MUST increment an ‘audit_event_dropped_total’ metric and include ‘audit_delivery_status=degraded’ in logs/telemetry. The platform provides at-least-once delivery when the pipeline is healthy; duplicates may occur.”

Hanae Suzuki: perfect, applying.

Olga Petrov: control mapping review notes:
- add explicit owner names for evidence collection (Security Eng vs SRE)
- for SOC2 CC7.2/CC7.3, include example “investigation trail” fields (event_id, actor_id, trace_id)
- keep query examples but label as “illustrative” (agree)

Hanae Suzuki: thanks. will update owners section and add the correlation field callout.

Elliot Price: editorial pass done. fixes:
- 3 broken links (ADR URL had old space key)
- inconsistent capitalization “schema version” vs “schema_version” in prose
- added a short “Terminology” section at top of schema page (actor vs subject vs target)

Hanae Suzuki: merged those edits. :pray:

Sahana Iyer: skimmed for CI/lint. suggest rewording:
From: “CI enforces required audit events for sensitive actions.”
To: “CI enforces schema validity + registered event_type for new/modified producers; coverage is verified via integration tests + telemetry dashboards.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1, updating.

Logan Wright: coverage doc looks good. one tweak: “coverage dashboard updates every 5 minutes” is too specific. we don’t want to lock that in. propose: “near-real-time (minutes) depending on telemetry pipeline.”

Hanae Suzuki: changed.

Naomi Feldman: product pass ok w/ one nit: avoid “SOC2 compliant” phrasing. changed to “supports SOC 2 / ISO-aligned controls” + “used as evidence for audits.”

Vivek Kulkarni: Private wording ok. i changed one sentence: “Private emits equivalent events where operator identity is available; if not available, actor_type=system and operator fields omitted.” avoids promising perfect operator attribution.

Hanae Suzuki: awesome. thanks all.

Hanae Suzuki: ✅ Status check: please react when your section is approved so i can publish.
- Marcus (schema/redaction): ?
- Allison (logging standard/delivery ADR): ?
- Olga (control mapping): ?
- Kevin (export mentions): ?
- Logan (coverage): ?
- Elliot (editorial/links): done
- Naomi (product wording): done
- Vivek (Private parity wording): done

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: schema/redaction approved after latest edits.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: approved.

Olga Petrov: :thumbsup: approved.

Kevin Osei: :thumbsup: approved (added dedupe blurb; pls ensure it’s consistent in both exporter + delivery ADR).

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: approved.

Hanae Suzuki: publishing now + will post announcement draft here before i send.

Hanae Suzuki: Announcement draft for #eng / #security-compliance (feedback welcome):
“New audit logging standards are live in Confluence: canonical schema v1, sensitive action taxonomy, coverage definition, versioning/backcompat, and redaction rules, plus ADRs for schema + delivery semantics. These docs define the required fields + event_type registry used across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. Please use the schema library + event_type registry for new producers; reach out in #eng-security for reviews/questions.”

Marcus Lin: looks good. add “do not log secrets” one-liner maybe.

Hanae Suzuki: will add. publishing + sending in the morning.

Marcus Lin: ty everyone. let’s make sure PR reviewers link back to these docs during rollout.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. closing thread.
